cram.def: Stops a threat BEFORE it occurs. Examples: firewall, lock, ACL, MFA.
Identifies threats AS they happen. Examples: IDS, CCTV, audit logs.
Reduces damage AFTER an incident and restores systems. Examples: backups, patches.
Discourages attackers without physically stopping them. Examples: warning signs, visible guards.
Alternative used when primary control can't be implemented. Examples: extra logging instead of MFA.
Instructs people on required behavior via policy. Examples: acceptable use policy, mandatory training.
Technology-based controls. Examples: firewalls, IDS/IPS, encryption, authentication systems.
Administrative/policy controls. Examples: risk assessments, security policies, security planning.
Controls carried out by people and procedures. Examples: guards, change management, IR drills.
Tangible real-world controls. Examples: locks, fences, mantraps, bollards, badge readers.
IDS = Detective (alerts only). IPS = Preventive (blocks traffic). Know this for the exam.

matching.prompt: CCTV monitoring a server room
Firewall blocking inbound traffic
Restoring from backup after ransomware
'No unauthorized access' warning sign
Extra logging when MFA unavailable
Mandatory password policy document
Encryption on a hard drive
Annual risk assessment process
Security guard patrolling perimeter

questions.q: Which control type is designed to stop a threat BEFORE it occurs?
opts: Detective
Corrective
Preventive
Compensating
questions.exp: Preventive controls stop threats before they happen — firewalls, ACLs, locks, MFA.

questions.q: A CCTV camera monitoring a server room is which control type?
opts: Preventive
Detective
Corrective
Directive
questions.exp: Detective controls identify and record events. CCTV logs intrusions but doesn't stop them.

questions.q: Extra logging added because MFA can't be funded is a _____ control.
opts: Corrective
Deterrent
Compensating
Physical
questions.exp: Compensating controls substitute for a primary control when it can't be implemented.

questions.q: A security guard patrolling a data center falls under which control CATEGORY?
opts: Technical
Managerial
Operational
Physical
questions.exp: Physical controls are tangible, real-world measures — guards, fences, locks, badge readers.

questions.q: Annual security awareness training is best classified as which control category?
opts: Technical
Managerial
Operational
Physical
questions.exp: Managerial controls are administrative — policies, risk assessments, security planning.

questions.q: A warning sign reading 'Unauthorized Access Prohibited' is primarily which control type?
opts: Preventive
Corrective
Deterrent
Directive
questions.exp: Deterrent controls discourage attackers by highlighting consequences without physically stopping them.

questions.q: IT restores from a clean backup after a malware infection. This is which control type?
opts: Preventive
Detective
Corrective
Compensating
questions.exp: Corrective controls minimize damage and restore systems after an incident.

questions.q: An IDS that alerts the SOC to suspicious traffic is which control type?
opts: Preventive
Detective
Corrective
Deterrent
questions.exp: An IDS detects and alerts only — unlike IPS it doesn't block, making it a detective control.

questions.q: A mantrap at a data center entrance is primarily which control type?
opts: Detective
Directive
Preventive
Corrective
questions.exp: A mantrap physically prevents unauthorized entry one person at a time — preventive.

questions.q: Which control category includes security guards, incident response procedures, and change management?
opts: Technical
Managerial
Operational
Physical
questions.exp: Operational controls are executed by people following procedures — guards, IR drills, change management.

questions.q: A policy stating 'All employees must wear ID badges' is which control type?
opts: Detective
Directive
Corrective
Compensating
questions.exp: Directive controls instruct personnel on required behavior through policy.

cram.def: The three foundational security goals: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
Only authorized users can access data. Controls: encryption, access controls, MFA, need-to-know.
Data has not been altered without authorization. Controls: hashing, digital signatures, permissions.
Systems and data accessible when needed. Controls: redundancy, backups, failover, load balancing.
Encryption scrambles data so only authorized parties can read it.
A hash changes if data is altered — used to verify data hasn't been tampered with.
DDoS attacks flood a system to make it unavailable — attacking availability.

matching.prompt: DDoS attack primary target
Encryption primarily protects
Hashing primarily protects
Attacker modifying records undetected
Hospital system offline during emergency
Unauthorized data disclosure
Redundant servers and backups support

questions.q: What does the CIA triad stand for?
opts: Control, Integrity, Access
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Cyber, Intelligence, Authority
Confidentiality, Identity, Access
questions.exp: CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three foundational goals of information security.

questions.q: Which CIA component ensures data is only accessible to authorized users?
opts: Integrity
Availability
Confidentiality
Authentication
questions.exp: Confidentiality protects data from unauthorized disclosure — encryption and access controls support this.

questions.q: A hospital's records system going offline during surgery violates which CIA component?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Non-repudiation
questions.exp: Availability ensures systems are accessible when needed — critical outages directly violate this.

questions.q: An attacker modifying financial records without detection violates which CIA component?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Authentication
questions.exp: Integrity ensures data has not been altered in an unauthorized way.

questions.q: Encryption primarily protects which CIA component?
opts: Integrity
Availability
Non-repudiation
Confidentiality
questions.exp: Encryption scrambles data so only authorized parties can read it — directly protecting confidentiality.

questions.q: A DDoS attack primarily targets which CIA component?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Authentication
questions.exp: DDoS floods a target to make it unavailable — directly attacking availability.

questions.q: Which of the following BEST supports availability?
opts: Encryption
Hashing
Redundant systems and backups
Access control lists
questions.exp: Redundancy and backups ensure services remain available even during failures or attacks.

questions.q: Hashing is primarily used to protect which CIA component?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Non-repudiation
questions.exp: Hashing verifies data hasn't changed — any modification produces a different hash, protecting integrity.

cram.def: Proof that an action occurred and the actor CANNOT deny it. Critical for legal/audit purposes.
Sender hashes the message, encrypts hash with their PRIVATE key. Provides authentication + integrity + non-repudiation.
You sign with YOUR private key. Only you have it — so only you could have signed it.
Anyone verifies your signature with YOUR public key. If valid, it proves you signed it.
HMAC uses shared key — both parties could have created it, so NO non-repudiation. Digital signatures use private key — only one party could sign.
A trusted authority certifies when a document was signed. Proves the document existed at that time.
Non-repudiation is closely related to integrity — you can't deny something that's cryptographically proven.

matching.prompt: Prevents a sender denying they sent a message
Key used to CREATE a digital signature
Key used to VERIFY a digital signature
Provides integrity + authentication but NOT non-repudiation
CIA component non-repudiation is most tied to
Technology that provides non-repudiation

questions.q: Non-repudiation ensures that:
opts: Data is encrypted in transit
A sender cannot deny sending a message
Only authorized users can read data
Systems remain available during attacks
questions.exp: Non-repudiation proves an action occurred and prevents the actor from denying it.

questions.q: Which technology provides non-repudiation?
opts: Symmetric encryption
Hashing alone
Digital signatures
VPN tunnels
questions.exp: Digital signatures use asymmetric cryptography to bind a message to its sender — providing non-repudiation.

questions.q: A digital signature is created using the sender's:
opts: Public key
Shared key
Private key
Session key
questions.exp: Senders sign with their PRIVATE key. Only they possess it, so only they could have signed.

questions.q: Non-repudiation is most closely related to which CIA component?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
All three equally
questions.exp: Non-repudiation is a property of integrity — implemented via digital signatures and tamper-evident logs.

questions.q: Why does HMAC NOT provide non-repudiation?
opts: It uses asymmetric keys
Both parties share the same key so either could have created it
It doesn't verify integrity
It is too slow
questions.exp: Non-repudiation requires that only one party could have created the proof. HMAC's shared key means either party could have.

questions.q: If Alice signs a message with her private key, what can Bob verify using her public key?
opts: The message is encrypted
Alice sent it and the content hasn't been altered
Bob's own identity
The message's availability
questions.exp: Bob verifying with Alice's public key confirms she signed it (authentication) and the content is intact (integrity).

questions.q: Which BEST supports non-repudiation in a corporate email system?
opts: TLS encryption on email transport
S/MIME digital signatures on emails
SPF records in DNS
Email archiving alone
questions.exp: S/MIME applies digital signatures to emails — binding the sender's identity to the message cryptographically.

cram.def: Verifying WHO you are. Methods: passwords, biometrics, tokens, certificates.
Determining WHAT you can do. Enforced via ACLs, RBAC, permissions after authentication.
Tracking WHAT YOU DID. Logs: login times, commands run, resources accessed. Critical for forensics.
Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service. Centralized AAA server for network access control.
Terminal Access Controller Access Control System Plus. Cisco-preferred AAA protocol. Separates AuthN, AuthZ, Accounting.
Knowledge factor: password, PIN, passphrase, security question.
Possession factor: smart card, hardware token, OTP app, phone.
Inherence/biometric factor: fingerprint, retina scan, facial recognition, voice.
Multi-Factor Authentication: uses two or more DIFFERENT factor types. Two passwords = not MFA.

matching.prompt: Verifying a user's identity at login
Granting access to a specific file share after login
Logging what commands a user ran
Centralized AAA server protocol
Smart card — which factor type?
Fingerprint scan — which factor type?
Password — which factor type?

questions.q: What does AAA stand for in network security?
opts: Authentication, Access, Auditing
Authentication, Authorization, Accounting
Authority, Access, Accountability
Assurance, Authorization, Auditing
questions.exp: AAA = Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), Accounting (what did you do?).

questions.q: Which AAA component verifies a user's identity?
opts: Authorization
Accounting
Authentication
Access control
questions.exp: Authentication answers 'who are you?' — verified via passwords, biometrics, tokens.

questions.q: Which AAA component determines what resources a user can access?
opts: Authentication
Accounting
Auditing
Authorization
questions.exp: Authorization answers 'what are you allowed to do?' — enforced via ACLs, roles, permissions.

questions.q: Which AAA component tracks what actions a user performed?
opts: Authentication
Authorization
Accounting
Administration
questions.exp: Accounting logs user activity — login times, commands run, resources accessed. Critical for forensics.

questions.q: RADIUS is primarily used for which function?
opts: Encrypting data at rest
Centralized AAA for network access
Generating digital certificates
Hashing passwords
questions.exp: RADIUS provides centralized AAA for network access control — widely used with 802.1X.

questions.q: Which of the following is 'something you have'?
opts: Password
Fingerprint
Hardware token
PIN
questions.exp: Hardware tokens and smart cards are possession factors — something you physically carry.

questions.q: A user provides a password AND a fingerprint. This is:
opts: Single-factor authentication
Two-factor authentication
Authorization
Identification
questions.exp: Two different factor types (knowledge + inherence) = two-factor authentication, a form of MFA.

questions.q: Using a PIN and a password together is:
opts: MFA because two factors are used
Not MFA because both are 'something you know'
Always sufficient for high-security systems
A form of biometric authentication
questions.exp: PIN and password are both knowledge factors — using two of the same factor type is NOT MFA.

cram.def: Identifies the difference (gap) between current security posture and a desired target state.
The target or desired state used for comparison. Often a security framework or regulatory requirement.
NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CIS Controls, PCI DSS, HIPAA.
A prioritized remediation roadmap listing what needs to be done to close each identified gap.
Pen test actively exploits. Gap analysis reviews documentation and controls against a framework.

matching.prompt: Current state vs desired state comparison
The target state used for comparison
Output of a gap analysis
NIST CSF or ISO 27001 used as
Gap analysis vs pen test — key difference
matching.answer: Gap analysis reviews controls; pen test actively exploits

questions.q: A gap analysis in security is used to:
opts: Actively exploit known vulnerabilities
Compare current security posture to a desired baseline
Monitor real-time network traffic
Classify data sensitivity levels
questions.exp: A gap analysis identifies the difference between where you are and where your security baseline requires you to be.

questions.q: Which document is commonly used as a baseline for a security gap analysis?
opts: A vulnerability scan report
A risk register
A security framework like NIST CSF or ISO 27001
A firewall ruleset
questions.exp: Security frameworks define desired security states that organizations compare their current posture against.

questions.q: After a gap analysis, the organization should produce:
opts: An immediate report to regulators
A prioritized remediation plan to close identified gaps
A full penetration test
A new firewall ruleset
questions.exp: The primary output of a gap analysis is a prioritized remediation roadmap.

questions.q: How does a gap analysis differ from a penetration test?
opts: Gap analysis is always automated
Gap analysis reviews controls against a standard; pen test actively exploits vulnerabilities
Gap analysis requires network access
They are the same thing
questions.exp: Gap analysis compares documentation and controls to a framework. Pen testing actively attempts exploitation.

cram.def: 'Never trust, always verify.' No implicit trust for any user, device, or network location.
Traditional perimeter model: inside the network = trusted. Zero Trust eliminates this assumption.
Divides the network into small isolated zones. Limits lateral movement if a segment is compromised.
Zero Trust continuously re-evaluates trust — not just at login. Considers device health, behavior, location.
Makes access decisions. Includes identity provider, policy engine, trust algorithm.
Enforces access decisions made by the control plane. Includes policy enforcement points (PEP).
The component that evaluates access requests against policy and grants or denies access.

matching.prompt: Core Zero Trust principle
Divides network into isolated zones
Makes access decisions in Zero Trust
Enforces access decisions in Zero Trust
Internal user treated how in Zero Trust?
matching.answer: Same as external — no implicit trust

questions.q: The core principle of Zero Trust is:
opts: Trust all internal network traffic
Never trust, always verify
Block all external traffic by default
Use VPNs for all connections
questions.exp: Zero Trust assumes no user or device is trusted by default — every access request must be verified.

questions.q: A Zero Trust model treats internal network users:
opts: As fully trusted by default
The same as external users — requiring verification
With higher trust than external users
As administrators by default
questions.exp: Zero Trust eliminates implicit trust for internal users — every request is authenticated and authorized.

questions.q: Micro-segmentation in a Zero Trust architecture is used to:
opts: Encrypt all data at rest
Divide networks into small isolated zones to limit lateral movement
Speed up authentication
Replace firewalls entirely
questions.exp: Micro-segmentation contains breaches — a compromised segment cannot easily reach other segments.

questions.q: Which BEST aligns with a Zero Trust approach?
opts: Allowing all intranet traffic without authentication
VPN granting full network access after login
Per-application MFA with continuous device health checks
Trusting devices on a known subnet
questions.exp: Zero Trust requires per-resource authentication with strong factors and continuous verification.

questions.q: Continuous verification in Zero Trust means:
opts: Passwords must be changed continuously
Trust is re-evaluated throughout a session, not just at login
All traffic is encrypted at all times
Users must re-authenticate every minute
questions.exp: Zero Trust continuously evaluates device health, user behavior, and context — not just at initial authentication.

cram.def: Two-door entry system — only one door open at a time. Prevents tailgating into secured areas.
Sturdy posts that prevent vehicles from ramming buildings or restricted areas.
'Something you have' physical access control. Logs who enters/exits and when.
Detective control. Deters and records — supports investigations after an incident.
Shielded enclosure that blocks electromagnetic signals — protects against RF attacks.
Requires two people present for sensitive operations — prevents insider threats in high-security areas.
Physically tether laptops and devices to fixed structures — prevents theft.

matching.prompt: Prevents tailgating with two-door entry
Prevents vehicle ramming attacks
Physical access token — 'something you have'
Blocks electromagnetic signals
Requires two people for sensitive operations
Physically secures a laptop to a desk

questions.q: A mantrap (access control vestibule) is designed to:
opts: Monitor video footage
Prevent tailgating by allowing only one person through at a time
Encrypt physical media
Alert the SOC of intrusions
questions.exp: A mantrap forces one-at-a-time entry through two interlocked doors — preventing tailgating.

questions.q: Which physical security device prevents vehicle-based attacks on buildings?
opts: Cable lock
Faraday cage
Bollard
Mantrap
questions.exp: Bollards are sturdy posts placed to stop vehicles from ramming into buildings or restricted areas.

questions.q: A Faraday cage is used to:
opts: Prevent unauthorized physical entry
Block electromagnetic signals to prevent RF eavesdropping
Log physical access events
Store encryption keys securely
questions.exp: A Faraday cage blocks electromagnetic fields — protecting against RF attacks and signal interception.

questions.q: Two-person integrity requires:
opts: Two passwords to log in
Two people present to perform sensitive operations
Two-factor authentication
Two security guards on patrol
questions.exp: Two-person integrity prevents insider abuse by requiring a witness for high-security operations.

questions.q: Motion-activated perimeter lighting is primarily which type of security control?
opts: Preventive
Detective
Deterrent
Corrective
questions.exp: Lighting deters intruders and improves camera footage — primarily a deterrent control.

cram.def: A single decoy system designed to attract attackers. Used to detect intrusions and study attacker techniques.
A network of honeypots simulating a realistic environment. Provides richer intelligence than a single honeypot.
A decoy file that alerts when accessed. No legitimate user would open it.
Fake data (credentials, records, API keys) that triggers an alert when used. Any access = intrusion.
Redirects malicious DNS queries to a controlled IP — neutralizes C2 (command and control) communication.
DETECTION and INTELLIGENCE — not blocking. Alert when attackers interact with decoys.

matching.prompt: Single decoy server to lure attackers
Network of decoy systems
Decoy file that alerts when opened
Fake credentials that alert when used
Redirects C2 DNS traffic to a controlled IP
Primary purpose of deception technologies

questions.q: A honeypot is best described as:
opts: A malware scanner
A decoy system designed to lure and study attackers
A firewall rule set
A password vault
questions.exp: Honeypots are intentionally vulnerable decoy systems that attract attackers so their techniques can be analyzed.

questions.q: Which deception technique redirects malicious DNS queries to neutralize C2 communications?
opts: Honeypot
DNS sinkhole
Honeytoken
Honeynet
questions.exp: DNS sinkholes intercept malicious domain lookups and redirect them to a controlled IP, cutting off C2 channels.

questions.q: A honeytoken is:
opts: A fake server
A decoy piece of data (credentials, file, API key) that alerts when accessed or used
A wireless access point
A type of firewall rule
questions.exp: Honeytokens are fake data items — any access reveals an attacker's presence since no legitimate use exists.

questions.q: The primary purpose of deception technologies is to:
opts: Block attacks immediately
Replace traditional firewalls
Detect attackers and gather intelligence about their techniques
Automatically patch vulnerabilities
questions.exp: Deception tech is about detection and intelligence — not blocking. Attackers interacting with decoys reveal themselves.

questions.q: Which deception technology would BEST help detect attackers who have already breached the perimeter?
opts: Firewall rules
Honeytokens and honeyfiles placed on internal systems
Perimeter IDS
DNS filtering
questions.exp: Internal honeytokens detect lateral movement — an attacker accessing them reveals they are inside.

cram.def: Controlled process to assess, approve, implement, and document IT changes — minimizing unintended security impacts.
Formal document describing a proposed change: what, why, impact, rollback plan, timeline.
Group of stakeholders who review and approve significant changes before implementation.
Pre-defined steps to revert to the previous working state if a change causes problems.
Period during which no changes are permitted — e.g. end of financial year, major events.
Pre-approved, low-risk, routine change that doesn't require full CAB review each time.
Urgent change (e.g. zero-day patch) — expedited approval process but still requires documentation.

matching.prompt: Formal document requesting a system change
Group that approves major changes
Plan to undo a failed change
Routine pre-approved low-risk change
Period when no changes are permitted

questions.q: The primary purpose of change management in IT security is to:
opts: Speed up software deployments
Ensure changes are assessed, approved, and documented to minimize risk
Replace vulnerability scanning
Automate patch deployment
questions.exp: Change management provides a controlled process for evaluating and approving changes to reduce unintended security impacts.

questions.q: A rollback plan is used to:
opts: Speed up deployments
Revert to the previous working state if a change fails
Archive old configurations
Train new staff
questions.exp: A rollback plan defines how to undo a change quickly if it causes problems, minimizing downtime.

questions.q: Which group reviews and approves significant changes before implementation?
opts: The CISO alone
Change Advisory Board (CAB)
The help desk
The penetration testing team
questions.exp: The CAB reviews proposed changes, assesses risk and impact, and approves or rejects before implementation.

questions.q: An emergency patch for a critical zero-day should:
opts: Skip all documentation since it's urgent
Follow an expedited change management process and be documented afterwards
Bypass testing entirely
Require no approval
questions.exp: Even emergency changes require an expedited process and post-implementation documentation.

questions.q: Which type of change is pre-approved, low-risk, and routine?
opts: Emergency change
Major change
Standard change
RFC
questions.exp: Standard changes are pre-approved routine changes that don't require CAB review every time.

questions.q: A change freeze is implemented because:
opts: All vulnerabilities have been patched
A high-risk period requires stability — no changes permitted
The CAB has not met recently
The rollback plan has failed
questions.exp: Change freezes prevent changes during critical business periods to reduce risk.

questions.q: An administrator changes a firewall rule without submitting an RFC or getting CAB approval. This is:
opts: Acceptable for minor changes
An unauthorized change that violates change management policy
Required in emergency situations
Standard practice
questions.exp: Unauthorized changes bypass review processes and can introduce vulnerabilities, outages, or compliance violations.

cram.def: Only approved applications can execute. Default-deny. Strongest application control.
Known bad applications are blocked. Default-allow. Less secure than allow listing.
Documented standard secure state of a system. Deviations are detected and remediated.
Tracks every change to code and configurations (git, SVN). Enables rollback, auditing.
Mirrors production — used to test changes safely before deploying to production.
Isolating an application or process to prevent it from affecting the rest of the system.
Instead of patching running systems, replace them with new pre-configured instances.

matching.prompt: Only approved software can execute
Known bad software is blocked, rest allowed
Standard secure configuration of a system
Tracks all changes to code over time
Safe environment for testing before production
Isolates an application to prevent system impact

questions.q: Which process ensures only approved software versions can execute in production?
opts: Change management
Allow listing
Vulnerability scanning
Penetration testing
questions.exp: Allow listing (whitelisting) ensures only explicitly approved software can execute — everything else is blocked.

questions.q: A configuration baseline is used to:
opts: Define maximum user permissions
Document and enforce the standard secure state of a system
Scan for vulnerabilities
Monitor network traffic in real time
questions.exp: A configuration baseline documents the standard secure configuration — deviations trigger alerts and remediation.

questions.q: Allow listing is considered more secure than block listing because:
opts: It requires less maintenance
It blocks everything by default and only permits approved applications
It is faster to implement
It does not require administrative rights
questions.exp: Allow listing is default-deny — only explicitly approved applications run. Block listing is default-allow, which is weaker.

questions.q: Sandboxing a suspicious file is used to:
opts: Encrypt it for secure storage
Execute it in an isolated environment to observe behavior without risking production systems
Delete it permanently
Send it to the antivirus vendor
questions.exp: Sandboxing isolates execution so any malicious behavior cannot affect the host system or network.

questions.q: An immutable infrastructure approach means:
opts: Systems are never updated
Instead of patching running systems, they are replaced with pre-configured new instances
All data is encrypted
Systems can never be changed
questions.exp: Immutable infrastructure replaces systems rather than patching them in place — eliminating configuration drift.

cram.def: Same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast. Problem: secure key exchange. Examples: AES, DES, 3DES.
Public/private key pair. Slower. Solves key exchange. Examples: RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman.
Shared freely. Used to ENCRYPT data sent TO you, and to VERIFY your digital signature.
Never shared. Used to DECRYPT data sent to you, and to CREATE your digital signature.
Public Key Infrastructure. System for managing digital certificates — issuance, renewal, revocation.
Trusted third party that issues, signs, and revokes digital certificates.
Top of the PKI hierarchy. Its certificate is self-signed. Trust anchor.
Subordinate CA that issues certificates on behalf of the root CA. Protects the root CA.
A copy of private keys stored securely with a third party — allows recovery if keys are lost.

matching.prompt: Same key for encryption and decryption
Public/private key pair
Key used to encrypt data sent TO you
Key used to decrypt data sent to you
Issues and signs digital certificates
Top of PKI hierarchy — self-signed cert
Stored copy of private keys with third party

questions.q: In asymmetric encryption, which key is used to encrypt data being sent to a recipient?
opts: The recipient's private key
The sender's private key
The recipient's public key
A shared symmetric key
questions.exp: Encrypt with the recipient's PUBLIC key — they then decrypt with their PRIVATE key.

questions.q: PKI is used to manage:
opts: Firewall rules
Digital certificates and key pairs
Password hashes
Network routing tables
questions.exp: PKI manages the lifecycle of digital certificates — creation, distribution, renewal, and revocation.

questions.q: Symmetric encryption is preferred for bulk data encryption because:
opts: It is more secure than asymmetric
It is significantly faster than asymmetric encryption
It does not require key exchange
It uses public/private key pairs
questions.exp: Symmetric algorithms (AES) are much faster than asymmetric (RSA) — used for bulk data; asymmetric used for key exchange.

questions.q: Which key must NEVER be shared in asymmetric cryptography?
opts: Public key
Session key
Private key
Shared key
questions.exp: The private key must never be shared — it is used to decrypt incoming data and to create digital signatures.

questions.q: An intermediate CA in a PKI hierarchy is used to:
opts: Self-sign root certificates
Issue certificates on behalf of the root CA, protecting the root from direct exposure
Store private keys
Revoke expired certificates
questions.exp: Intermediate CAs issue certificates while the root CA remains offline — protecting the critical trust anchor.

questions.q: Key escrow means:
opts: Destroying old keys after rotation
Storing a copy of private keys with a trusted third party for recovery purposes
Encrypting keys with a master key
Publicly publishing private keys
questions.exp: Key escrow allows recovery of encrypted data if keys are lost — required in some regulated industries.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of asymmetric encryption?
opts: AES-256
3DES
RSA
ChaCha20
questions.exp: RSA is asymmetric (public/private key pair). AES, 3DES, and ChaCha20 are all symmetric algorithms.

cram.def: Stored data — on drives, databases, USB, backups. Encrypted with AES-256 typically.
Data moving across a network. Protected by TLS, HTTPS, VPN, SSH.
Data actively being processed in memory/CPU. Hardest to protect. Secure enclave helps.
Encrypts entire storage device. BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (macOS). Protects stolen devices.
Protects data in transit. Replaced SSL. Used by HTTPS (port 443).
Advanced Encryption Standard. Symmetric block cipher. Standard for data at rest. AES-256 strongest.
Destroying the encryption key makes encrypted data permanently unreadable — secure data deletion method.

matching.prompt: Encrypted data stored on a hard drive
TLS protects data in which state
Data being processed in RAM
BitLocker encrypts the entire drive
Standard encryption algorithm for data at rest
Destroying the key to make data unreadable

questions.q: Data encrypted while stored on disk is called:
opts: Data in transit
Data in use
Data at rest
Data in process
questions.exp: Data at rest refers to stored data — on drives, databases, and backups. Typically encrypted with AES.

questions.q: TLS protects data in which state?
opts: At rest
In use
In transit
In backup
questions.exp: TLS encrypts data moving across networks — protecting data in transit between clients and servers.

questions.q: Full disk encryption (FDE) primarily protects against:
opts: Network eavesdropping
Unauthorized access to data when a device is lost or stolen
Malware execution
Phishing attacks
questions.exp: FDE encrypts the entire drive — stolen devices cannot have their data read without the decryption key.

questions.q: Which state of data is most difficult to protect with encryption?
opts: Data at rest
Data in transit
Data in use
Data in backup
questions.exp: Data in use (in RAM/CPU) is actively being processed — it must be decrypted to be used, making it hard to protect.

questions.q: Cryptographic erasure is:
opts: Overwriting data multiple times
Physically destroying storage media
Destroying the encryption key to render encrypted data permanently unreadable
Deleting the file system
questions.exp: Cryptographic erasure destroys only the key — the encrypted data remains but is permanently inaccessible.

questions.q: Which protocol replaced SSL and is now used to protect HTTPS traffic?
opts: SSH
TLS
IPsec
SFTP
questions.exp: TLS (Transport Layer Security) replaced SSL and is the standard for encrypting web traffic — HTTPS uses TLS.

cram.def: How do you securely share a symmetric key when you have no secure channel yet? Diffie-Hellman solves this.
Allows two parties to derive a shared secret over a public channel without transmitting the secret itself.
Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral. Generates new key pair for each session — provides Perfect Forward Secrecy.
Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral. Stronger security with smaller key sizes. Used in modern TLS.
Each session uses a unique ephemeral key. Compromising the long-term private key doesn't expose past sessions.

matching.prompt: Solves secure key exchange over public channel
Unique key per session — provides PFS
Past sessions protected even if private key compromised
DH variant using elliptic curves — smaller keys
Older key exchange — no PFS

questions.q: Diffie-Hellman is used to:
opts: Sign digital certificates
Hash passwords
Establish a shared secret over an insecure public channel
Encrypt email attachments
questions.exp: Diffie-Hellman allows two parties to derive a shared secret over a public channel without transmitting the secret.

questions.q: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) ensures that:
opts: Encryption never expires
Compromising the long-term private key does not expose past session keys
All keys are stored permanently
One key is used for all sessions
questions.exp: PFS uses ephemeral session keys — even if the server's private key is later stolen, past sessions remain safe.

questions.q: Why is RSA key exchange being phased out of modern TLS in favor of DHE/ECDHE?
opts: RSA is too slow for modern hardware
RSA key exchange does not provide Perfect Forward Secrecy
RSA uses symmetric keys
RSA cannot encrypt data
questions.exp: RSA key exchange uses the same server private key for all sessions — compromising it exposes all past sessions. DHE/ECDHE provide PFS.

questions.q: DHE stands for:
opts: Dynamic Hash Encryption
Diffie-Hellman Exchange
Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral
Distributed Hash Encryption
questions.exp: DHE = Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral. 'Ephemeral' means a new key pair is generated for each session — enabling PFS.

cram.def: Hardware chip on motherboard. Stores encryption keys, certificates, passwords. Tied to specific hardware. Used by BitLocker.
Dedicated enterprise device for secure key generation, storage, and cryptographic operations.
TPM = built into a single device. HSM = external/network device, enterprise-grade, high-performance.
Isolated CPU region for sensitive operations. Apple Secure Enclave, ARM TrustZone, Intel SGX.
Storage device with built-in encryption hardware. Encryption/decryption happens at drive level.

matching.prompt: Hardware chip on motherboard storing crypto keys
Enterprise external device for key management
Isolated CPU region — resistant to OS compromise
BitLocker relies on this hardware component
Drive with built-in encryption hardware

questions.q: A TPM (Trusted Platform Module) is used to:
opts: Provide internet access
Securely store cryptographic keys and support hardware-based encryption tied to a device
Block malware at the network level
Manage user passwords in Active Directory
questions.exp: TPM is a hardware chip that stores keys, certificates, and passwords — bound to the specific device it's installed in.

questions.q: BitLocker relies on which hardware component to verify boot integrity before releasing the encryption key?
opts: HSM
UEFI only
TPM
Smart card exclusively
questions.exp: BitLocker uses the TPM to seal the volume encryption key — it checks system integrity at boot before decryption.

questions.q: A secure enclave is:
opts: A physically locked server room
An isolated processor area that protects sensitive code and data from the main operating system
A type of VPN tunnel
An encrypted backup location
questions.exp: Secure enclaves are isolated execution environments resistant to OS-level attacks.

questions.q: Which of the following provides the BEST protection for private keys in a high-security enterprise environment?
opts: Storing keys in an encrypted file on a server
TPM on each workstation
A dedicated HSM
Keeping keys in a password manager
questions.exp: HSMs are the gold standard for enterprise key protection — tamper-resistant, high-performance, with strict access controls.

cram.def: Hiding secret data WITHIN ordinary non-secret files (images, audio, video). The existence of the message is concealed.
Replaces sensitive data with a random non-sensitive token. Original data stays in a secure vault. Used in PCI DSS.
Replaces real data with realistic-looking fake data for dev/test environments. Original data preserved.
Encryption mathematically secures data. Obfuscation hides it. Both can be used together.
Payment card data — token used in transactions, real card number never stored by merchant.
Developers need realistic data for testing without using real customer PII.

matching.prompt: Hiding a message inside an image file
Replacing a credit card number with a random token
Showing fake but realistic data to developers
Token maps to real data stored in secure vault
Original data preserved but not shown

questions.q: Steganography is the practice of:
opts: Encrypting data with a strong key
Hiding secret data within ordinary non-secret files such as images or audio
Hashing passwords before storage
Generating digital certificates
questions.exp: Steganography hides messages inside ordinary files — the existence of the secret message is itself concealed.

questions.q: Tokenization replaces sensitive data with:
opts: An encrypted version of the data
A random non-sensitive token that maps back to the original in a secure vault
A hash of the data
A compressed version of the data
questions.exp: Tokenization substitutes sensitive data (e.g. credit card numbers) with a random token — the original stays in a secure vault.

questions.q: Data masking is used to:
opts: Permanently delete sensitive data
Replace real data with realistic-looking fake data so developers can test without real PII
Encrypt backups
Monitor who accesses data
questions.exp: Data masking provides realistic test data without exposing real customer information to developers or testers.

questions.q: How does steganography differ from encryption?
opts: Steganography is always stronger
Steganography hides the existence of the message; encryption hides its content
They are the same technique
Encryption is illegal; steganography is not
questions.exp: Encryption makes the message unreadable. Steganography hides the fact that a message exists at all.

cram.def: One-way (can't reverse), deterministic (same input = same hash), avalanche effect (small change = completely different hash).
128-bit hash. BROKEN — collision vulnerabilities. Not used for security. Still used for checksums.
256-bit hash. Part of SHA-2 family. Current standard for most security applications.
Random value added to a password BEFORE hashing. Unique per user. Defeats rainbow tables.
Precomputed hash-to-plaintext mapping. Defeated by salting passwords.
Hash-based Message Authentication Code. Hash + shared secret key. Provides integrity + authentication.
1. Hash the message. 2. Encrypt hash with private key. 3. Recipient decrypts with public key. 4. Compare hashes.

matching.prompt: 128-bit hash — broken, collision vulnerabilities
256-bit hash — current standard
Random value added before hashing passwords
Precomputed hash table — defeated by salts
Hash + shared secret for integrity + authentication
Small input change produces completely different hash
Step 1 of creating a digital signature

questions.q: Which hashing algorithm produces a 256-bit hash and is considered the current standard?
opts: MD5
SHA-1
SHA-256
SHA-512
questions.exp: SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest and is part of the SHA-2 family — widely used and considered secure.

questions.q: What is added to a password BEFORE hashing to defend against rainbow table attacks?
opts: IV (Initialization Vector)
Pepper
Salt
Nonce
questions.exp: A salt is a unique random value added per user before hashing — making precomputed rainbow table attacks useless.

questions.q: Why is MD5 no longer recommended for security purposes?
opts: It is too slow
It produces collision vulnerabilities — different inputs can produce the same hash
It uses asymmetric keys
It requires a password to compute
questions.exp: MD5 has known collision vulnerabilities — two different inputs can produce the same hash, breaking integrity guarantees.

questions.q: A digital signature is verified using the sender's:
opts: Private key
Shared key
Public key
Session key
questions.exp: The sender's PUBLIC key is used to verify their signature — anyone can verify, but only the private key holder could have signed.

questions.q: HMAC provides which security properties?
opts: Encryption and compression
Integrity and authentication using a shared secret key
Non-repudiation and confidentiality
Key exchange and digital signatures
questions.exp: HMAC combines hashing with a shared secret — verifying both that the data hasn't changed and that the sender knows the key.

questions.q: What is the correct order for creating a digital signature?
opts: Encrypt the message, then hash it
Hash the message, then encrypt the hash with the sender's private key
Hash the message, then encrypt it with the recipient's public key
Encrypt with symmetric key, then sign with public key
questions.exp: Digital signing: hash the message → encrypt the hash with your private key. Verification: decrypt with public key → compare hashes.

cram.def: Distributed, immutable ledger. Each block contains data + hash of previous block. Tampering breaks the chain.
Copies exist on many nodes simultaneously. No single point of failure or control.
Once recorded, blockchain data cannot be altered without invalidating all subsequent blocks.
How nodes agree on valid transactions. Examples: Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS).
Blockchain primarily provides INTEGRITY and NON-REPUDIATION. Not confidentiality by default.

matching.prompt: Primary security property blockchain provides
What makes blockchain tamper-evident
matching.answer: Each block hashes the previous block

matching.prompt: Copies on many nodes simultaneously
Once written, cannot be changed without detection
How nodes agree on valid transactions

questions.q: A blockchain is best described as:
opts: A centralized encrypted database
A distributed immutable ledger where each block contains a hash of the previous block
A type of symmetric encryption
A certificate authority
questions.exp: A blockchain is a distributed ledger — each block cryptographically links to the previous, making tampering detectable.

questions.q: What makes blockchain records tamper-evident?
opts: They are stored with AES-256 encryption
Each block contains a hash of the previous block — altering any block invalidates all subsequent blocks
They use symmetric keys for signing
Records are deleted after 30 days
questions.exp: Altering any block changes its hash, which invalidates the next block's stored hash — breaking the entire chain.

questions.q: Which security property does blockchain primarily provide?
opts: Confidentiality
Integrity and non-repudiation
Availability
Authentication
questions.exp: Blockchain's cryptographic chaining provides strong integrity — records cannot be altered undetected.

cram.def: Binds a public key to an identity. Signed by a CA. Contains: subject, public key, issuer, validity dates.
Standard format for digital certificates. Used in TLS, S/MIME, code signing.
Generated by requester. Contains public key + identity info. Sent to CA for signing.
Domain Validation. Only proves ownership of domain. Fastest to obtain. Basic HTTPS.
Extended Validation. Rigorous identity check. Highest assurance.
*.example.com covers ALL first-level subdomains. One cert for many subdomains.
Subject Alternative Name. Single cert covers MULTIPLE different domain names.
Published list of revoked certificates. Clients download and check against it.
Real-time certificate revocation checking. Client queries OCSP responder for specific cert status.
Server includes OCSP response in TLS handshake — reduces client lookup latency.
Application hardcodes expected certificate/public key. Rejects any other cert — prevents MITM.
Signed by its own private key (not a CA). Browsers show warnings. Used in internal/dev environments.

matching.prompt: Only proves domain ownership — fastest
Highest assurance — rigorous organization verification
Single cert covering all subdomains of a domain
Single cert covering multiple different domains
Real-time revocation checking protocol
Published list of revoked certificates
Server includes OCSP response in TLS handshake
App hardcodes expected certificate to prevent MITM
Document sent to CA requesting certificate issuance
Signed by itself — not trusted by browsers by default

questions.q: Which protocol provides REAL-TIME certificate revocation status checking?
opts: CRL
LDAP
OCSP
SCEP
questions.exp: OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) allows clients to query the revocation status of a specific certificate in real time.

questions.q: A wildcard certificate (*.example.com) covers:
opts: Only example.com itself
All first-level subdomains of example.com
Multiple unrelated domains
Only internal IP addresses
questions.exp: A wildcard cert covers any first-level subdomain: mail.example.com, www.example.com, app.example.com, etc.

questions.q: A self-signed certificate is signed by:
opts: A trusted root CA
An intermediate CA
The entity that owns it, using its own private key
A registration authority
questions.exp: Self-signed certs are signed by the entity itself — not a trusted third-party CA, so browsers display warnings.

questions.q: Certificate pinning is used to:
opts: Speed up TLS handshakes
Prevent MITM attacks by hardcoding the expected certificate or public key in an application
Automatically renew certificates
Generate CSRs
questions.exp: Certificate pinning rejects any certificate not matching the hardcoded expected value — even a valid CA-signed cert.

questions.q: What is a CSR (Certificate Signing Request)?
opts: A revocation notice sent to a CA
A document generated by the requester containing their public key and identity info, sent to a CA for signing
A type of firewall rule
A hash of a certificate
questions.exp: A CSR is generated by the requester — it contains their public key and identifying info, and is sent to a CA to be signed.

questions.q: OCSP stapling improves performance by:
opts: Caching certificates on the client
Having the server include the OCSP revocation response in the TLS handshake, eliminating a separate client lookup
Compressing certificate data
Reducing the key size
questions.exp: OCSP stapling has the server provide the pre-fetched OCSP response during the handshake — no extra round trip for the client.

cram.def: Government-backed. Most resources, most sophisticated. Motivated by espionage, disruption, or strategic advantage.
Ideologically motivated. Goal: disruption or publicity for a cause. Examples: Anonymous.
Financially motivated. Organized crime. Ransomware, fraud, data theft for resale.
Low skill. Uses pre-made tools without understanding them. Opportunistic, unsophisticated.
Current or former employee/contractor with legitimate access who abuses it. Intentional or unintentional.
Corporate espionage. Steals IP, trade secrets, or business intelligence.
Long-term, stealthy, targeted campaign. Usually nation-state or organized crime. Maintains persistent access.
Unauthorized hardware/software used inside an organization without IT approval — creates unmanaged risk.

matching.prompt: Government-backed, most sophisticated
Ideologically motivated, wants publicity
Primarily motivated by financial gain
Uses tools they don't understand
Has legitimate access and abuses it
Long-term stealthy targeted campaign
Unsanctioned technology used without IT approval

questions.q: Which threat actor typically has the most resources and sophisticated capabilities?
opts: Unskilled attacker
Hacktivist
Nation-state
Insider threat
questions.exp: Nation-state actors have government backing, large budgets, and the most advanced TTPs.

questions.q: A hacker defacing websites to protest corporate environmental practices is a:
opts: Nation-state actor
Unskilled attacker
Hacktivist
Cybercriminal
questions.exp: Hacktivists are motivated by ideology or political causes — not financial gain.

questions.q: Which threat actor uses pre-written tools and exploits without truly understanding them?
opts: APT
Hacktivist
Insider threat
Unskilled attacker
questions.exp: Unskilled attackers rely on existing tools — low skill, opportunistic, often causing unintended damage.

questions.q: An employee stealing customer data before resigning is which type of threat actor?
opts: Hacktivist
Insider threat
Nation-state
Unskilled attacker
questions.exp: Insider threats have legitimate access and abuse it — making them uniquely dangerous.

questions.q: APT stands for:
opts: Advanced Phishing Tactic
Advanced Persistent Threat
Automated Penetration Tool
Application Protection Technology
questions.exp: APT = Advanced Persistent Threat — long-term stealthy campaigns, typically nation-states or organized crime.

questions.q: Financial gain is the PRIMARY motivation for which threat actor?
opts: Hacktivist
Nation-state
Cybercriminal
Unskilled attacker
questions.exp: Cybercriminals are primarily financially motivated — ransomware, fraud, data theft for resale.

questions.q: What most distinguishes an insider threat from external attackers?
opts: High technical skill level
Financial motivation
Legitimate existing access to systems and data
Exclusive use of zero-day exploits
questions.exp: Insider threats already have authorized access — bypassing perimeter controls that stop external attackers.

questions.q: Shadow IT refers to:
opts: Malware that hides from antivirus software
Unauthorized hardware or software used inside an organization without IT approval
A category of nation-state actor
Dark web forums used by attackers
questions.exp: Shadow IT creates unmanaged risks — IT can't patch, monitor, or secure what it doesn't know exists.

questions.q: An APT differs from a typical cyberattack in that:
opts: APTs always use physical access
APTs are short and loud, designed to cause immediate damage
APTs involve long-term, stealthy access designed to persist undetected
APTs only target individuals
questions.exp: APTs are designed for persistence — the goal is long-term access for espionage or sabotage.

scenarios.q: A company's finance director receives an email appearing to come from the CEO requesting an urgent $150,000 wire transfer to a new vendor account. The CEO is traveling and unreachable by phone. What type of threat actor technique is MOST likely being used?
opts: A hacktivist disrupting financial operations
A cybercriminal conducting Business Email Compromise
A nation-state actor conducting espionage
An insider threat exploiting travel absence
scenarios.exp: BEC (Business Email Compromise) is a financially motivated cybercriminal technique — impersonating executives to authorize fraudulent wire transfers. Urgency, travel context, and financial request are classic BEC indicators.

scenarios.q: A security analyst notices that a recently terminated employee's VPN credentials were used to access internal systems three days after termination, downloading 40GB of files. Which threat actor category does this BEST represent?
opts: Nation-state actor using stolen credentials
Unskilled attacker exploiting a default account
Insider threat — a former employee exploiting delayed de-provisioning
Hacktivist conducting data disclosure
scenarios.exp: Former employees whose access isn't immediately revoked remain insider threats post-termination. The 3-day window is a de-provisioning failure — the most dangerous insider threat window.

scenarios.q: An organization's SOC detects a low-and-slow intrusion active for 8 months. The attacker has pivoted through 12 systems, established multiple backdoors, and exfiltrated intellectual property in small encrypted chunks. No ransom demand has been made. Which threat actor profile BEST fits?
opts: Unskilled attacker using automated scanning tools
Cybercriminal conducting ransomware staging
Nation-state APT conducting long-term espionage
Hacktivist building public embarrassment
scenarios.exp: APT characteristics: long dwell time, multiple persistence mechanisms, intellectual property focus, no financial demand, slow exfiltration. Nation-state actors prioritize persistence over speed.

scenarios.q: A developer installs an unapproved cloud storage app on their work laptop to share large files with clients. IT discovers the app is syncing internal project documents to a personal cloud account. The developer had no malicious intent. Which risk category BEST describes this?
opts: Insider threat — deliberate data exfiltration
Shadow IT creating an unmanaged data exposure risk
Nation-state supply chain attack via the cloud app
Cybercriminal malware disguised as a productivity tool
scenarios.exp: Shadow IT: the developer isn't malicious — they're solving a real problem. But IT can't monitor, control, or secure tools they don't know exist. The exposure is real even without malicious intent.

scenarios.q: Security researchers discover a threat group has been compromising industrial control systems at energy utilities across three countries over two years, causing minor undetected calibration errors that could eventually cause equipment failures. No financial demand has been made. This MOST closely resembles:
opts: Organized cybercriminals staging for ransomware
Hacktivists protesting energy policy
Unskilled attackers using script-based automation
A nation-state actor with strategic infrastructure disruption objectives
scenarios.exp: Nation-state characteristics: multi-year campaign, critical infrastructure target, geographic scope, sabotage without financial motive, and operational technology focus. This aligns with state-sponsored strategic disruption objectives.

cram.def: The path or method an attacker uses to gain unauthorized access to a system.
The total set of points (vectors) where an attacker can try to enter or extract data.
Disable unnecessary services, close unused ports, remove default accounts, patch vulnerabilities.
Email, SMS, IM — used for phishing, malware delivery, social engineering.
Malicious macros in Office documents, malicious PDFs, infected executables.
Infected USB drives, external drives — baiting attacks.
Factory-set admin/admin — must be changed immediately on all devices.
Compromising software or hardware before it reaches the target — e.g. SolarWinds.

matching.prompt: Path an attacker uses to gain access
All points where an attacker can enter
admin/admin left unchanged on a router
Malware injected into a software update
Infected USB drive left in car park
Malicious macro in Word document

questions.q: A threat vector is best defined as:
opts: A type of malware
The path or method an attacker uses to gain unauthorized access to a system
A vulnerability severity score
A type of firewall rule
questions.exp: A threat vector is the route through which an attacker accesses or attacks a target system.

questions.q: Reducing the attack surface involves:
opts: Adding more security monitoring tools
Eliminating unnecessary services, closing unused ports, removing default accounts
Increasing user permissions for productivity
Deploying more honeypots
questions.exp: Reducing attack surface removes unnecessary entry points — fewer ways in means less risk.

questions.q: Default credentials on a newly deployed network device represent which type of vulnerability?
opts: Physical attack vector
Supply chain vulnerability
Configuration-based attack vector
Social engineering vector
questions.exp: Default credentials (admin/admin) are a well-known configuration weakness — change them on first login.

questions.q: Which threat vector involves malicious code injected into a legitimate software update before delivery?
opts: Phishing vector
Supply chain attack
Social engineering
Insider threat vector
questions.exp: Supply chain attacks inject malware into legitimate software or hardware before it reaches the target — e.g. SolarWinds.

questions.q: Which of the following BEST reduces the risk from open service ports?
opts: Installing a honeypot on each open port
Disabling unnecessary services and closing unused ports
Monitoring port traffic passively
Requiring stronger passwords for service accounts
questions.exp: Closing unused ports and disabling unnecessary services eliminates those attack vectors entirely.

scenarios.q: A penetration tester hired by a retail company finds that the company's customer-facing web application, employee VPN portal, a forgotten staging server, and 3 IoT-connected security cameras are all accessible from the internet. The tester flags this before attempting exploitation. What concept does this BEST illustrate?
opts: A large attack surface with multiple potential entry points
A single critical vulnerability requiring immediate patching
A misconfigured firewall allowing all inbound traffic
Shadow IT created by the security camera vendor
scenarios.exp: Attack surface is the sum of all points where an attacker could try to enter. Four separate internet-facing systems — especially the forgotten staging server and IoT cameras — represent unnecessary exposure that should be reduced.

scenarios.q: A new branch office deploys 20 switches. The IT team configures VLANs and firewall rules but forgets to change default admin credentials. Six months later, an attacker gains admin access using credentials from the vendor's publicly available setup guide. Which threat vector did the attacker exploit?
opts: An unpatched firmware vulnerability in the switch OS
Default credentials left unchanged — a misconfiguration vector
A supply chain attack on the switch firmware
Physical access to the switch management ports
scenarios.exp: Default credentials are a configuration-based threat vector. The attacker didn't need to find a vulnerability — the organization handed over access by failing the most basic hardening step.

scenarios.q: A security team receives an alert that malware was found on 12 workstations. All affected machines opened the same vendor invoice PDF sent via email two weeks ago. The PDF exploited an unpatched vulnerability in the PDF reader. Which combination of threat vectors does this attack chain use?
opts: Removable media vector leading to network propagation
Message-based vector (email) delivering a file-based payload
Voice-based vector followed by a web-based exploit
Supply chain vector through the vendor's email system
scenarios.exp: Two-stage vector chain: email (message-based vector) as the delivery mechanism, with a malicious PDF (file-based vector) as the payload. The PDF exploited a client-side vulnerability — a classic phishing-to-file-exploit chain.

scenarios.q: A security architect reviewing internet-facing services finds: a public website, a customer portal, an API used by mobile apps, an RDP server used by remote admins, a legacy FTP server no longer actively used, and an exposed Elasticsearch database with no authentication. What is the MOST effective first action without purchasing new tools?
opts: Deploy an IDS to monitor all six services
Patch the public website and customer portal first
Disable or remove the legacy FTP server and the unauthenticated Elasticsearch instance — reducing the attack surface
Require MFA for the RDP server only
scenarios.exp: Attack surface reduction: removing unnecessary services eliminates the attack vector entirely. The legacy FTP server and unauthenticated Elasticsearch are the highest-risk, lowest-justification exposures — they shouldn't exist at all.

scenarios.q: An attacker sends a malicious Word document to 500 employees via a spoofed vendor email. The document contains a macro that downloads a RAT from a compromised website and establishes a C2 connection. Three employees enable the macro. Which combination of threat vectors is used?
opts: Message-based → file-based → web-based
Voice-based → removable media → network
Supply chain → insider threat → physical
File-based → DNS-based → wireless
scenarios.exp: Multi-vector chain: email (message-based) delivers the Word doc (file-based), the macro downloads from a compromised site (web-based), establishing C2. Understanding how vectors combine is key to the exam.

cram.def: Mass fraudulent emails impersonating trusted entities to steal credentials or install malware.
Targeted phishing at a specific person or group. Personalized to seem more legitimate.
Spear phishing targeting senior executives (CEO, CFO, CISO). High-value targets.
Voice phishing — social engineering over phone calls. Often impersonates IT support or banks.
SMS phishing — malicious links or requests sent via text message.
Redirects users to a fake website even if they type the correct URL — DNS poisoning or hosts file modification.
Sender Policy Framework. DNS record listing authorized sending mail servers. Prevents spoofed sender addresses.
DomainKeys Identified Mail. Cryptographic email signature. Proves email came from the domain.
Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance. Builds on SPF + DKIM.

matching.prompt: Mass email impersonating a bank
Targeted email at a specific individual
Targeted at a CEO or CFO
Phishing via phone call
Phishing via SMS text message
Redirects correct URL to fake site
DNS record listing authorized sending mail servers
Cryptographic email signature per domain
Policy for handling SPF/DKIM failures

questions.q: A highly targeted phishing email sent to a CEO is called:
opts: Spear phishing
Whaling
Smishing
Vishing
questions.exp: Whaling targets high-value executives. Spear phishing is targeted but at a broader group.

questions.q: SMS-based phishing is called:
opts: Vishing
Whaling
Smishing
Spear phishing
questions.exp: Smishing = SMS phishing — malicious links or requests delivered via text message.

questions.q: Vishing is phishing conducted via:
opts: Email
SMS text message
Voice phone calls
Social media direct messages
questions.exp: Vishing uses phone calls — attackers often impersonate IT support, financial institutions, or government agencies.

questions.q: Pharming differs from phishing in that:
opts: Pharming uses email; phishing uses DNS
Pharming redirects users to fake sites even when they type the correct URL
Pharming only targets executives
Pharming requires physical access
questions.exp: Pharming attacks DNS or the hosts file — users are silently redirected to a fake site even after typing the correct address.

questions.q: Which email security standard publishes a list of authorized sending mail servers in DNS?
opts: DKIM
DMARC
SPF
TLS
questions.exp: SPF (Sender Policy Framework) publishes authorized sending servers in DNS — receiving servers reject mail from others.

questions.q: DKIM provides which security property for email?
opts: Encrypts the email body
Cryptographically signs the email to verify it came from the domain and wasn't altered in transit
Lists authorized sending servers
Blocks phishing emails automatically
questions.exp: DKIM applies a cryptographic signature to emails — verifying the sending domain and detecting tampering.

questions.q: DMARC builds on which two standards?
opts: TLS and SSL
SPF and DKIM
DNSSEC and HTTPS
MFA and SSO
questions.exp: DMARC extends SPF and DKIM — defining what to do (reject, quarantine, report) when emails fail both checks.

scenarios.q: An employee receives an email warning that their Office 365 account will be suspended in 24 hours unless they verify credentials immediately. The link goes to a convincing Microsoft login replica. The employee's manager received the same email. What type of attack is this, and what email control would MOST directly have blocked it?
opts: Spear phishing; blocked by DKIM signing of outbound email
Mass phishing; blocked by SPF/DMARC rejecting email spoofing Microsoft's domain
Whaling; blocked by requiring MFA on the email account
Vishing; blocked by caller ID verification
scenarios.exp: Mass phishing (not targeted) impersonating Microsoft. SPF/DMARC rejects spoofed sender domains — if Microsoft has DMARC p=reject, receiving mail servers reject email falsely claiming to be from microsoft.com.

scenarios.q: The CFO receives a phone call from someone claiming to be from the company's external auditors, requesting network credentials to run a remote audit tool. The caller knows the CFO's name, the auditor firm's name, and the upcoming audit date. What attack is being attempted, and what should the CFO do?
opts: Smishing attack — hang up and report to IT security
Vishing attack — verify the caller's identity through an independently sourced phone number before providing anything
BEC attack — forward the request to finance for processing
Watering hole attack — avoid clicking any links the caller sends
scenarios.exp: Vishing with pretexting: social engineering via voice call. The attacker did reconnaissance to build credibility. The correct response is independent verification — hang up, find the auditor's number from an official statement, and call back.

scenarios.q: A security awareness trainer sends a simulated phishing email about a new benefits portal to all 2,000 employees. 340 click the link; 89 enter credentials. Most clickers are warehouse division employees who rarely use computers. What is the MOST appropriate next action?
opts: Immediately terminate the 89 employees who entered credentials
Deploy SPF and DKIM on the mail server
Provide targeted security awareness training to the warehouse division with role-appropriate examples
Implement MFA organization-wide and consider the matter resolved
scenarios.exp: The simulation identified a specific vulnerable population. MFA is important but doesn't address why they clicked — they need training tailored to their context and how they actually use technology.

scenarios.q: A company's DMARC policy is set to p=none. The security team wants to change it to p=reject, but the IT director warns this could break email from third-party marketing tools. What does p=none mean in practice, and what is the risk of leaving it?
opts: p=none blocks all failing email — it's already the most secure setting
p=none only monitors and reports failures without blocking anything — spoofed email claiming to be from the company's domain is still delivered
p=none quarantines suspicious email to spam folders
p=none requires SPF but not DKIM
scenarios.exp: p=none is monitoring-only — no enforcement. Attackers can still send spoofed email from your domain and it will be delivered. The IT director's concern is valid, but the solution is to fix misconfigured senders and move to p=quarantine then p=reject.

scenarios.q: Employees at a hospital begin receiving texts claiming the payroll portal requires immediate password reset due to a 'security incident.' Two employees reset their passwords on the fake site. What is the IMMEDIATE priority for the security team?
opts: Pharming attack — the DNS server needs to be flushed
Smishing attack — immediately reset the compromised credentials and enable MFA on those accounts, then investigate what was accessed
Vishing attack — block the phone number used
Spear phishing — identify which executives were targeted
scenarios.exp: Smishing (SMS phishing). Immediate priority is containing the damage: reset the two compromised accounts, force MFA, and check for any access using those credentials before the security team arrived. Stopping active harm precedes investigation.

cram.def: Creating a fabricated scenario (pretext) to manipulate a victim. 'I'm from IT, I need your password.'
Fake websites, emails, or apps mimicking trusted brands (Microsoft, Apple, banks) to steal credentials.
Attacker impersonates an executive via email to trick employees into wire transfers or credential disclosure.
Registering domain names similar to legitimate ones (gooogle.com) to catch mistyped URLs.
Using stolen personal information to impersonate someone for financial gain or access.

matching.prompt: Fabricating a scenario to extract information
Fake Microsoft login page to steal credentials
Posing as an executive via email for wire transfer
Domain gooogle.com registered to catch typos

questions.q: Pretexting involves:
opts: Sending malicious email attachments
Fabricating a believable scenario to manipulate a victim into providing information or access
Installing keyloggers remotely
Cracking encrypted passwords
questions.exp: Pretexting creates a believable false context — the attacker plays a role to make the manipulation seem legitimate.

questions.q: Business Email Compromise (BEC) attacks primarily target:
opts: Personal email accounts
Executives and finance staff to authorize fraudulent wire transfers or reveal credentials
Network infrastructure
Web application databases
questions.exp: BEC impersonates executives via email to manipulate employees — often resulting in large fraudulent wire transfers.

questions.q: Typosquatting involves:
opts: Injecting malicious code into legitimate websites
Registering domain names similar to legitimate ones to catch users who mistype URLs
Spoofing email sender addresses
Cloning RFID badges
questions.exp: Typosquatting registers slightly misspelled versions of popular domains — capturing misdirected traffic.

scenarios.q: A new employee receives a call from 'IT helpdesk' saying there's a critical security issue with their laptop and asking them to install a remote access tool. The employee didn't report any issue. What attack is this, and what should the employee do?
opts: A legitimate IT helpdesk call — install the tool as instructed
A quid pro quo social engineering attack — hang up and verify by calling the IT helpdesk directly using the number from the company intranet
A vishing attack seeking to install ransomware — report to the police immediately
A baiting attack — the 'tool' is a USB drive the attacker will mail later
scenarios.exp: Quid pro quo: attacker offers IT help in exchange for the employee installing remote access software. Verify independently — hang up and call IT using a number from a trusted internal source, not one the caller provides.

scenarios.q: A financial institution's customers are being redirected to a fake banking site even when they type the correct URL directly into the browser. The institution's own DNS records appear correct. What is MOST likely occurring?
opts: The institution's SPF record is misconfigured, allowing spoofed emails
Pharming — the attacker has poisoned a widely-used upstream DNS resolver's cache
A typosquatting attack targeting customers who misspell the URL
An SSL stripping attack downgrading customer connections to HTTP
scenarios.exp: Pharming via DNS cache poisoning — the correct URL is being typed but a compromised resolver is returning a malicious IP. The institution's own DNS records being correct rules out domain hijacking.

scenarios.q: The CEO posts on LinkedIn that she is attending a conference in Singapore next week. Three days later, the CFO receives an email from the CEO's personal email requesting he authorize an emergency $80,000 gift card purchase for a client deal that must close before the CEO returns. What attack is this, and what control would MOST directly prevent it?
opts: Spear phishing via email — SPF on the CEO's personal email would block it
BEC/whaling exploiting public OSINT — a callback verification policy requiring voice confirmation for financial requests above a threshold
Vishing pretending to be the CEO — caller ID authentication
Supply chain attack through the conference organizer
scenarios.exp: Whaling/BEC using OSINT: the LinkedIn post provided the travel window. The CEO's personal email can't be controlled by corporate SPF. The control that matters is a human process: any financial request above threshold requires a live voice callback using a known number.

scenarios.q: Threat intelligence reveals attackers have registered 'microsofft.com', 'rniicrosoft.com', and 'microsoft-security-alert.com' — all hosting convincing Microsoft login pages. What technique is being used, and which population is MOST at risk?
opts: DNS poisoning — all internet users
Typosquatting and brand impersonation — users who mistype Microsoft's URL or click links without checking the full domain
Watering hole attacks — Microsoft employees specifically
BGP hijacking — enterprise customers with direct Microsoft connections
scenarios.exp: Typosquatting (microsofft, rniicrosoft) catches fast typists. Brand impersonation (microsoft-security-alert.com) catches users who click links in emails without inspecting the full URL. Users who carefully verify domains before entering credentials are protected.

scenarios.q: An attacker spends two weeks building rapport with a target company's IT administrator on a professional networking site, posing as a cybersecurity consultant. After establishing trust, they send the admin a 'security assessment tool' to run on the network — actually a backdoor. What social engineering principle is being exploited?
opts: Scarcity — the attacker created urgency to act quickly
Authority — posing as a cybersecurity expert
Liking — building genuine rapport before the malicious request to reduce scrutiny
Intimidation — threatening professional consequences for non-compliance
scenarios.exp: Liking: people lower their defenses and comply more readily with people they like. Two weeks of rapport-building is deliberate — it makes the malicious request feel like a favor to a friend rather than a suspicious request from a stranger.

cram.def: Attacker compromises a website frequently visited by the target group. Victims infect themselves by visiting a site they trust.
Users trust their regular sites. Doesn't require direct contact with victims. Hard to detect before infection.
Another name for watering hole attack — compromising strategically chosen sites.
Patch browsers/plugins promptly. Web content filtering. Threat intelligence feeds. Network monitoring.

matching.prompt: Compromising a site frequented by the target group
Why victims don't suspect the attack
matching.answer: They trust the site they're visiting

matching.prompt: Another name for a watering hole attack

questions.q: A watering hole attack involves:
opts: Flooding a server with traffic to cause denial of service
Compromising a website frequently visited by the target group to infect their systems
Intercepting network traffic with a MITM attack
Sending targeted phishing emails to specific individuals
questions.exp: Watering hole attacks compromise sites the target regularly visits — the attacker waits for victims to come to them.

questions.q: Why are watering hole attacks difficult to detect before infection?
opts: They exclusively use zero-day exploits
They target only government systems
The compromised site appears legitimate and is one the victim already trusts
They always use encrypted communication
questions.exp: Victims have no reason to distrust a familiar, legitimate-looking site — the malicious code is hidden within it.

questions.q: A threat actor wants to target engineers at an energy company. They compromise a popular ICS/SCADA industry news website. This is:
opts: Spear phishing
Brand impersonation
A watering hole attack
A supply chain attack
questions.exp: Compromising an industry site frequented by the target group — waiting for them to visit — is a watering hole attack.

scenarios.q: An attacker wants to target engineers at a specific aerospace company without sending any direct communications. They research which industry forums and technical news sites the engineers regularly visit, then compromise one of those sites by injecting malicious JavaScript that exploits a browser vulnerability. Three weeks later, four engineers' workstations are compromised. What attack technique was used?
opts: Spear phishing campaign targeting the engineers directly
Supply chain attack on the engineering software vendor
Watering hole attack — compromising sites the target group frequents rather than contacting targets directly
Baiting attack using USB drives left at the conference
scenarios.exp: Watering hole: the attacker never contacted the targets. They compromised trusted sites the targets visit — exploiting the target's own browsing behavior. The engineers had no reason to distrust their regular professional resources.

scenarios.q: A financial firm's SOC detects malware on three analyst workstations. All three visited a well-known financial regulation news website last Tuesday. The website appears legitimate and is still online. Threat intel confirms the site was briefly compromised with a malicious iframe last Tuesday afternoon. What MOST directly would have prevented the infection?
opts: Blocking the financial regulation website entirely
Deploying a network IDS to alert on the malicious iframe
Ensuring browser and PDF reader plugins were fully patched — the exploit targeted an unpatched browser vulnerability
Implementing DMARC on the company's email domain
scenarios.exp: Watering hole attacks exploit client-side vulnerabilities. Fully patched browsers often survive watering hole attempts because the exploits target specific known CVEs. Blocking the site wasn't possible in advance. Network IDS alerts but doesn't prevent.

scenarios.q: A red team is tasked with compromising a government agency. Direct phishing is detected and blocked by their email gateway. The red team identifies three external contractor websites that agency staff regularly use for project collaboration and compromises one. Which offensive technique is this?
opts: Spear phishing via a third-party email system
