opts: Malware that always executes immediately on installation
Malicious code that lies dormant and executes only when a specific trigger condition is met
A type of DDoS attack
A hardware vulnerability
questions.exp: Logic bombs trigger on conditions like a specific date, a user logging in, or a file being deleted — often planted by disgruntled insiders.

questions.q: Why are rootkits particularly difficult to detect?
opts: They use strong encryption
They operate at the kernel level and intercept OS calls — hiding themselves from the very tools used to detect them
They change their code with each infection
They only infect BIOS firmware
questions.exp: Rootkits modify how the OS reports system information — making infected files, processes, and connections invisible to standard tools.

scenarios.q: A forensic analyst discovers a script embedded in a legitimate backup software installation that monitors whether a specific IT administrator account is still active in Active Directory. If the account is removed, the script triggers and deletes critical database backups. The script was planted by a contractor 14 months ago. What type of malware is this?
opts: A rootkit — it hid itself for 14 months
Ransomware staged for activation at a specific time
A logic bomb — malicious code that executes when a specific trigger condition is met (the admin account being deleted)
A backdoor — the contractor used it to maintain access after the contract ended
scenarios.exp: Logic bomb: the trigger condition is the removal of a specific account. This is a classic insider threat technique — planting a logic bomb before departure. Detection is difficult because the code is dormant until triggered. Code review of all contractor-written code is the mitigation.

scenarios.q: A user reports their computer is extremely slow. Investigation reveals: CPU pegged at 95-100%, the culprit process is named 'svchost.exe' but its path is %AppData% rather than System32, and it's making outbound connections to multiple external IPs. No ransom demand has been made and files appear intact. What is MOST likely occurring?
opts: A legitimate Windows update running in the background
Ransomware encrypting files — the ransom demand hasn't appeared yet
A cryptominer — hijacking CPU resources to mine cryptocurrency, disguised as svchost.exe but running from an unusual path
A worm propagating across the network
scenarios.exp: Cryptominer indicators: maxed CPU, no file encryption, outbound connections, disguised as a legitimate process name but running from an unexpected path (%AppData% instead of System32). Cryptominers need the CPU for mining — no interest in ransoming or destroying data.

cram.def: Copying the data from an RFID badge/card to create a duplicate. Used to bypass card-based access controls.
Device attached to card readers (ATMs, POS) to capture card data.
Searching rubbish bins for sensitive information — documents, hardware, sticky notes with passwords.
Physically observing someone entering credentials or viewing sensitive information.

matching.prompt: Copying RFID badge data to make a clone
Device on ATM capturing card data
Searching bins for sensitive documents
Watching someone type their password

questions.q: RFID cloning is used to:
opts: Intercept wireless network traffic
Copy the data from an RFID access card to create a duplicate that bypasses physical access controls
Block RFID signals
Encrypt RFID communications
questions.exp: RFID cloning captures and duplicates the data on proximity cards — the cloned card can then be used to bypass access controls.

questions.q: Dumpster diving is a physical attack that involves:
opts: Filling a server room with water
Searching through discarded material for sensitive information like documents or hardware
Overloading physical security systems
Dropping infected USB drives in car parks
questions.exp: Organizations discard sensitive documents without shredding — dumpster diving recovers this information. Mitigated by shredding policies.

questions.q: Shoulder surfing is best mitigated by:
opts: Using stronger passwords
Privacy screens on monitors and shielding PIN entry from observation
Encrypting all data
Implementing MFA
questions.exp: Privacy screens limit the viewing angle of displays. Shielding PIN entry prevents visual interception.

scenarios.q: A penetration tester walks into a financial firm's building alongside a group of employees returning from lunch, and proceeds to an unlocked conference room where they access an unattended laptop. They printed official-looking visitor badges with a major consulting firm's logo. What TWO physical attack techniques did the tester use?
opts: Shoulder surfing and RFID cloning
Tailgating (following employees through access control without authenticating) and impersonation (fake visitor badge from a known vendor)
Dumpster diving and baiting
Skimming and social engineering via phone
scenarios.exp: Two techniques: (1) Tailgating — followed the legitimate employee group through access control. (2) Impersonation — the fake badge from a recognized firm reduced scrutiny. Mitigations: mantrap (prevents tailgating), verify all visitors at reception regardless of badge, enforce clean desk/screen lock policy.

scenarios.q: An attacker attaches a device to an ATM's card reader that stores magnetic stripe data from every card inserted. A small camera captures PIN entry. Three weeks later, the attacker creates cloned cards. What attacks were used and what mitigations protect against this?
opts: RFID cloning and shoulder surfing — mitigated by Faraday cages
Card skimming and physical camera for PIN capture — mitigated by chip-and-PIN cards (chip cannot be cloned from the stripe), PIN pad shields, and ATM physical inspection programs
Vishing and impersonation — mitigated by caller ID authentication
Bluetooth sniffing and keylogging — mitigated by disabling Bluetooth on ATMs
scenarios.exp: Skimming and PIN camera capture. Chip-and-PIN (EMV) was specifically designed to defeat skimming — the chip generates a unique transaction code each time and cannot be cloned from the magnetic stripe. PIN pad shields make camera capture harder. Banks run ATM inspection programs to detect skimmers.

cram.def: Attack from multiple compromised sources (botnet) — much harder to block by IP.
Floods network bandwidth — UDP floods, ICMP floods, DNS amplification. Measured in Gbps.
Exhausts server connection tables — SYN flood exploits TCP three-way handshake.
Targets specific web application — HTTP flood, Slowloris. Hard to distinguish from legitimate traffic.
Sends many SYN packets without completing handshake — exhausts server connection table (half-open connections).
Small request → large response reflected at victim. DNS/NTP amplification. Multiplies attack bandwidth.
Attacks hardware/firmware directly — 'bricks' the device permanently. Also called phlashing.

matching.prompt: DoS from many compromised sources
Floods bandwidth with UDP/ICMP
TCP handshake exploit leaving half-open connections
Small request produces large response at victim
Permanently damages hardware via firmware attack

questions.q: How does a DDoS attack differ from a basic DoS attack?
opts: DDoS always uses encryption
DDoS originates from many distributed sources (botnet), making it much harder to block by IP
DDoS only targets web applications
DDoS requires physical access
questions.exp: DDoS uses thousands of compromised systems as sources — blocking one IP has no effect, unlike a single-source DoS.

questions.q: A SYN flood attack works by:
opts: Flooding a target with large UDP packets
Sending many TCP SYN packets without completing the three-way handshake, exhausting the server's connection table
Amplifying traffic using DNS servers
Disrupting application-layer HTTP requests
questions.exp: SYN floods leave many half-open TCP connections — eventually the server's connection table is full and it can't accept legitimate connections.

questions.q: A DNS amplification attack is effective because:
opts: DNS servers are always unpatched
Small DNS query requests produce much larger responses that are reflected at the victim — multiplying attacker bandwidth
DNS uses TCP which is easier to flood
DNS responses are not encrypted
questions.exp: DNS amplification: attacker sends small spoofed DNS queries; large responses go to the victim — amplifying bandwidth by 50-70x.

scenarios.q: An organization's e-commerce website becomes unavailable during a major sale event. Analysis shows the web servers are receiving 50,000 requests per second — 40x normal — all appearing to be legitimate HTTP GET requests for the product catalog. The requests come from 200,000 different IP addresses globally. What type of attack is this, and why is IP blocking ineffective?
opts: A volumetric DDoS using UDP flood — block all UDP traffic
An application layer DDoS (HTTP flood) — IP blocking is ineffective because the attack uses 200,000 distributed source IPs (a botnet). Blocking individual IPs doesn't scale
A SYN flood — increase the SYN backlog queue on web servers
A DNS amplification attack — block all DNS traffic
scenarios.exp: Application layer DDoS: HTTP requests look legitimate. IP blocking is ineffective against a 200,000-IP botnet — each IP contributes minimally but collectively overwhelm the server. Mitigations: rate limiting per IP, challenge-response (CAPTCHA), CDN/DDoS scrubbing service, and geo-IP filtering if the sale is region-specific.

scenarios.q: A SYN flood sends 100,000 TCP SYN packets per second without completing the three-way handshake. After 30 seconds, the server's connection table is full and it cannot accept legitimate connections. What mitigation technique specifically addresses the resource exhaustion aspect of SYN floods?
opts: Increase the server's RAM to hold more half-open connections
SYN cookies — the server doesn't allocate resources until the handshake is completed, encoding state cryptographically into the initial SYN-ACK response
Block all inbound SYN packets — this prevents legitimate connections too
Rate limit TCP connections to 10 per second from any single IP
scenarios.exp: SYN cookies: instead of allocating state for each SYN, the server encodes connection information into the ISN of the SYN-ACK. No memory is allocated until the ACK arrives completing the handshake. Legitimate clients complete the handshake; attackers' spoofed packets never do — eliminating resource exhaustion.

cram.def: Corrupting a DNS server's cache with false records — redirecting users to attacker-controlled sites.
Attacker gains control of a domain registration — changes DNS records to redirect all traffic.
Redirecting malicious domain lookups to a controlled IP — blocks malware C2 communication.
DNS Security Extensions — cryptographically signs DNS records to prevent poisoning/spoofing.
Hiding data exfiltration or C2 traffic within DNS queries — often overlooked by firewalls that allow DNS.

matching.prompt: Corrupting DNS cache with false records
Attacker controls domain registration
Cryptographic signing of DNS records
Data hidden in DNS queries for exfiltration
Redirecting malware C2 DNS to controlled IP

questions.q: DNS cache poisoning works by:
opts: Flooding a DNS server with requests
Inserting false DNS records into a resolver's cache — causing users to be redirected to attacker-controlled IP addresses
Intercepting DNS traffic with a MITM attack
Registering similar domain names
questions.exp: DNS cache poisoning corrupts cached records — any user that resolver serves will be redirected to the attacker's IP.

questions.q: DNSSEC mitigates DNS attacks by:
opts: Encrypting DNS query content
Cryptographically signing DNS records so resolvers can verify their authenticity
Blocking all external DNS queries
Requiring MFA for DNS changes
questions.exp: DNSSEC uses digital signatures on DNS records — resolvers verify the signature, detecting tampered or forged records.

questions.q: DNS tunneling is used to:
opts: Speed up DNS resolution
Hide data exfiltration or C2 traffic within DNS queries — often passing through firewalls that allow DNS
Attack DNS servers directly
Poison DNS caches
questions.exp: DNS tunneling encodes data within DNS queries — many firewalls allow all DNS traffic, making it a covert channel.

scenarios.q: Employees report that when they visit the company's banking portal (internalbank.company.com), they're seeing a certificate error and the site looks different. Investigation reveals the DNS resolver used by workstations is returning an incorrect IP for internalbank.company.com. Internal DNS zone files are correct. What attack is occurring?
opts: Domain hijacking — the attacker has taken over the domain registration
DNS cache poisoning of an upstream or internal resolver — the resolver has been fed false records. DNSSEC would allow resolvers to verify the authenticity of DNS responses
A watering hole attack on the banking portal itself
BGP hijacking redirecting traffic before DNS resolution
scenarios.exp: DNS cache poisoning: the resolver's cache has been poisoned with false records. Internal zone files being correct rules out domain hijacking. DNSSEC cryptographically signs zone data — a resolver can verify the DNS response came from the authoritative source. Certificate errors on the fake site are a detection opportunity — train users to report them.

scenarios.q: A security team finds an endpoint making hundreds of DNS queries per hour for subdomains of a domain with no web presence. The subdomains are all long random-looking strings (a3f7b2c1d8e4.attacker.com, b9d2e7f3a1c4.attacker.com). DNS responses return valid IPs but no traffic goes to those IPs. What is most likely happening?
opts: The endpoint has malware constantly scanning for open ports
DNS tunneling — the endpoint is exfiltrating data encoded in DNS query subdomains, with responses potentially carrying C2 commands back
A worm using DNS to propagate through random subdomain generation
Legitimate CDN behavior generating random subdomain lookups
scenarios.exp: DNS tunneling: data is encoded in subdomain labels of DNS queries. The 'random' subdomains are actually encoded data. Responses carry C2 commands back. Most firewalls allow DNS — attackers exploit this. Detection: anomalously high DNS query volume, long/random subdomain strings, queries for domains with no legitimate web presence.

cram.def: Rogue access point mimicking a legitimate SSID. Victims connect and attacker intercepts traffic (MITM).
Sending forged 802.11 deauth frames to disconnect clients from legitimate AP.
Capturing the 4-way handshake during authentication for offline brute-force cracking.
Brute-forcing WPS 8-digit PIN. Only 11,000 combinations needed (design flaw). Disable WPS.
Transmitting radio frequency interference to disrupt wireless communications.

matching.prompt: Rogue AP with same SSID as legitimate network
Forged 802.11 frames to disconnect clients
Brute-forcing WPS 8-digit PIN

questions.q: An evil twin attack involves:
opts: Stealing a WPA2 handshake
Creating a rogue access point that mimics a legitimate network's SSID to intercept victim traffic
Jamming wireless signals to disrupt communications
Brute-forcing a WPA2 password
questions.exp: Evil twin APs mimic legitimate SSIDs — clients connect thinking they're on the real network, enabling MITM interception.

questions.q: WPS vulnerabilities are best mitigated by:
opts: Using WPA3 exclusively
Hiding the network SSID
Disabling WPS on all access points
Using a stronger WPS PIN
questions.exp: WPS has a fundamental design flaw — the 8-digit PIN can be brute-forced in ~11,000 attempts. Disable it entirely.

questions.q: Capturing a WPA2 4-way handshake allows an attacker to:
opts: Immediately gain network access
Perform offline brute-force or dictionary attacks against the pre-shared key
Decrypt all previously captured network traffic
Inject traffic into the wireless network
questions.exp: The captured handshake contains enough information for offline cracking — the attacker can test passwords without further network interaction.

scenarios.q: During a wireless assessment, a tester sets up a rogue AP with the same SSID as 'CorpWiFi' with a stronger signal than legitimate APs. Employee laptops automatically connect to the rogue AP. The tester intercepts all their traffic. What attack is this, and what would have prevented automatic connection?
opts: A deauthentication attack — prevented by management frame protection
An evil twin attack — prevented by requiring WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X) which validates the server certificate, so clients refuse to connect to APs presenting unexpected certificates
A WPS PIN attack — prevented by disabling WPS on corporate APs
A Bluetooth pairing attack — prevented by requiring PIN authentication
scenarios.exp: Evil twin: WPA2-Personal (PSK) has no way for clients to verify the AP's identity. WPA2-Enterprise with EAP-TLS or PEAP requires the AP to present a certificate signed by a trusted CA — a rogue AP without that certificate is rejected. Enterprise wireless should use 802.1X, not PSK.

scenarios.q: A wireless penetration tester uses aireplay-ng to send deauthentication frames to clients, forcing them to reconnect. The tester captures the 4-way handshake and takes it offline for dictionary attack. What is the tester doing, and what wireless standard was designed to address the handshake capture vulnerability?
opts: Performing a WPS PIN attack — WPA3 removes WPS entirely

Capturing a WPA2 4-way handshake via forced deauthentication for offline cracking — WPA3 with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) addresses this by providing forward secrecy; even capturing the exchange doesn't enable offline cracking
Performing a Bluetooth attack — Bluetooth 5.0 addresses the handshake vulnerability
Conducting ARP poisoning on the wireless network
scenarios.exp: WPA2 handshake capture: deauth forces reauthentication, the 4-way handshake contains enough information for offline password cracking. WPA3's SAE replaces the PSK exchange — it's a zero-knowledge proof that prevents offline cracking even with a full capture of the authentication exchange.

cram.def: Attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties — intercepting and potentially modifying traffic.
Sends fake ARP replies associating the attacker's MAC with a legitimate IP — redirecting LAN traffic through attacker.
Downgrades HTTPS to HTTP — attacker sees plaintext traffic. Requires MITM position.
HTTP header instructing browsers to ALWAYS use HTTPS — mitigates SSL stripping.
Attacker advertises false BGP routes — redirecting internet traffic through attacker-controlled infrastructure.

matching.prompt: Attacker positioned between two communicating parties
Fake ARP replies redirect LAN traffic to attacker
Downgrading HTTPS to HTTP
HTTP header forcing HTTPS always
False BGP route advertisements redirect internet traffic

questions.q: An on-path (man-in-the-middle) attack allows an attacker to:
opts: Crash a target system
Intercept and potentially read or modify communications between two parties without their knowledge
Enumerate open network ports
Scan for wireless networks
questions.exp: MITM attacks give the attacker full visibility of — and ability to modify — the traffic between victim and server.

questions.q: ARP poisoning enables an on-path attack by:
opts: Flooding the network with ARP requests
Sending fake ARP replies that associate the attacker's MAC address with a legitimate IP — redirecting LAN traffic through the attacker
Cracking ARP encryption
Blocking all ARP traffic
questions.exp: ARP poisoning corrupts the ARP cache of victims — they send traffic to the attacker thinking it's going to the legitimate destination.

questions.q: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) mitigates SSL stripping by:
opts: Encrypting all cookies
Instructing browsers to always use HTTPS for a domain — refusing to downgrade to HTTP
Blocking non-HTTPS traffic at the firewall
Requiring certificate pinning
questions.exp: HSTS tells browsers the site must always be accessed via HTTPS — browsers refuse to connect over HTTP even if redirected.

scenarios.q: A user can access their bank account fine at home, but on public WiFi the bank's website shows HTTP instead of HTTPS and their credentials may have been captured. Later they find unauthorized transactions. What attack likely occurred, and what should the bank have implemented?
opts: DNS poisoning at the coffee shop's router — DNSSEC would have prevented this

SSL stripping — the attacker used a MITM position to intercept the initial HTTP request and prevent upgrade to HTTPS. HSTS with preloading would have caused the browser to refuse HTTP connections to the bank domain
A rogue certificate authority — certificate pinning would have prevented this
Packet sniffing of unencrypted WiFi — WPA3 with OWE would have encrypted the WiFi traffic
scenarios.exp: SSL stripping on public WiFi: HSTS tells browsers 'always use HTTPS for this domain' — preloaded HSTS means even the first connection is forced to HTTPS before any server response. No HTTP connection is ever made, nothing to strip.

scenarios.q: A penetration tester sends gratuitous ARP replies claiming their MAC address is associated with the default gateway's IP. Within seconds, all workstations send traffic to the tester instead of the actual gateway. The tester forwards traffic to the real gateway while recording it. What attack is this, and what Layer 2 control prevents it?
opts: DNS cache poisoning — DNSSEC prevents this
IP spoofing — firewall egress filtering prevents this
ARP poisoning/spoofing — Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches prevents it by validating ARP packets against a trusted DHCP snooping database
BGP hijacking — route filtering on border routers prevents this
scenarios.exp: ARP poisoning: ARP has no authentication. Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) is the Layer 2 control: the switch inspects ARP packets and compares them against the DHCP snooping binding table. Gratuitous ARP replies that don't match legitimate bindings are dropped.

cram.def: Capturing valid authentication or data packets and retransmitting them to gain unauthorized access.
Capturing an NTLM password hash and replaying it for authentication — no need to crack the hash.
Stealing an authenticated session token to impersonate a user without their credentials.
Number used Once. Random value added to authentication to prevent replay — each session unique.

matching.prompt: Retransmitting captured auth packets for access
Using captured NTLM hash directly for authentication
Stealing authenticated session token
Random value preventing replay of old authentication

questions.q: A replay attack works by:
opts: Guessing authentication credentials through brute force
Capturing and retransmitting valid authentication packets to gain access without needing the actual credentials
Injecting malicious code into authentication packets
Cracking encrypted session keys
questions.exp: Replay attacks capture valid auth packets and replay them — the system accepts them as legitimate since they passed authentication originally.

questions.q: Pass-the-hash is a type of replay attack because:
opts: It cracks the hash before using it
The captured NTLM hash is replayed directly for authentication — no need to know the actual password
It requires the attacker to be on the same network
It only works against web applications
questions.exp: Pass-the-hash uses the captured hash as the authentication credential — replaying it to gain access as the victim.

questions.q: Nonces prevent replay attacks by:
opts: Encrypting all authentication traffic
Adding a unique random value to each authentication request — old requests with invalid nonces are rejected
Requiring biometric authentication
Limiting login attempts
questions.exp: Each nonce is unique and single-use — replaying an old authentication packet fails because the nonce has already been used.

scenarios.q: An attacker captures network traffic during a user's authentication to a web application and records the session cookie. An hour later, the attacker injects the captured cookie into their own browser and gains access. What attack occurred and what implementation choices would have prevented it?
opts: A CSRF attack — prevented by CSRF tokens

Session hijacking via cookie theft — prevented by: Secure flag (HTTPS-only transmission), HttpOnly flag (no JavaScript access), short session timeout, and binding sessions to the client's IP or browser fingerprint
A replay attack on the password hash — prevented by NTLM challenge-response
A SQL injection providing direct database access
scenarios.exp: Session hijacking: the cookie was captured in transit (Secure flag would have prevented transmission over HTTP). The attack succeeded because the cookie has no expiry context or binding — it works anywhere. Session binding and short timeouts limit the replay window.

scenarios.q: A Windows domain uses NTLM authentication for legacy applications. An attacker compromises one workstation and uses Mimikatz to extract NTLM hashes from memory. They then authenticate to a file server using only the captured hash — without cracking it. What technique is this, and what architectural change most effectively prevents it?
opts: Credential stuffing — prevented by MFA

Pass-the-hash — most effectively prevented by transitioning from NTLM to Kerberos, implementing Protected Users security group to prevent hash caching, and restricting lateral movement via network segmentation
A replay attack on Kerberos tickets — prevented by short ticket lifetimes
Mimikatz itself is the vulnerability — prevented by patching Mimikatz signatures in AV
scenarios.exp: Pass-the-hash exploits NTLM's design — the hash IS the credential. Mitigations: Kerberos doesn't have the same hash-replay issue. Protected Users group prevents NTLM fallback and credential caching. Windows Credential Guard stores hashes in an isolated environment. Network segmentation limits what the attacker can reach even with valid hashes.

cram.def: Malicious script uploaded to a web server giving attacker remote command execution via HTTP.
Malicious VBA macro embedded in Office documents. Executes when document is opened and macros enabled.
Using legitimate system tools (PowerShell, WMI, certutil) for malicious purposes — evades detection.
Initial malware that downloads and installs the main malicious payload. First stage of multi-stage attack.

matching.prompt: Malicious script uploaded to web server for remote access
Malicious VBA code in Office documents
Using legitimate system tools maliciously
Initial malware that downloads main payload

questions.q: A web shell is:
opts: A secure remote administration tool
A malicious script uploaded to a web server that gives an attacker remote command execution through HTTP
A type of cross-site scripting attack
A firewall configuration for web servers
questions.exp: Web shells provide persistent remote access to a compromised web server — the attacker issues commands via HTTP requests.

questions.q: 'Living off the land' (LOLBins) attacks involve:
opts: Installing custom malware tools
Using legitimate, built-in system tools like PowerShell and WMI for malicious purposes to evade detection
Physical access to victim systems
Social engineering via phone calls
questions.exp: LOLBins use trusted system tools — since they're legitimate applications, many security tools don't flag their malicious use.

questions.q: A dropper malware's primary function is:
opts: Encrypting files for ransom
Serving as the initial payload that downloads and installs the main malicious component
Exfiltrating data directly
Providing remote desktop access
questions.exp: Droppers are first-stage malware — they establish a foothold and then download the main payload from C2 infrastructure.

scenarios.q: A security analyst reviews web server logs and notices hundreds of POST requests to /admin/shell.php — a URL not in the application's code. Each POST contains a command in the body (ls, whoami, cat /etc/passwd) and responses contain the command output. What has likely occurred?
opts: The web server has a SQL injection vulnerability being exploited
A web shell has been uploaded to the server — the attacker is executing OS commands through HTTP requests to the malicious script
A CSRF attack is manipulating server-side commands
The web application has a directory traversal vulnerability
scenarios.exp: Web shell: the attacker previously uploaded a PHP script to the web server — likely via a file upload vulnerability. The shell accepts commands via POST and executes them with the web server's privileges. Incident response: isolate the server, find how the shell was uploaded, remove it, identify what was accessed.

scenarios.q: A threat hunter finds that a legitimate scheduled task on several workstations runs: powershell.exe -enc [long base64 string]. The base64 decodes to a script downloading and executing files from an external domain. What technique is being used and why is it effective at evading detection?
opts: A macro virus embedded in Office documents — effective because Office macros are trusted

Living-off-the-land using PowerShell with encoded commands via a scheduled task — effective because PowerShell is a legitimate admin tool, base64 encoding evades simple string-based detection, and the scheduled task provides persistence without a persistent file
A rootkit modifying the Windows task scheduler
A worm using legitimate system tools to propagate
scenarios.exp: LOLBins + encoded commands + scheduled task persistence: all three use legitimate Windows features. PowerShell is expected on corporate endpoints. Base64 encoding hides the payload from string-matching rules. Scheduled tasks are legitimate persistence mechanisms. Detection requires PowerShell ScriptBlock logging (which decodes base64) and monitoring for scheduled task creation events.

cram.def: Using ../ sequences to navigate outside the intended web directory — accessing system files.
Tricks authenticated users into unknowingly submitting requests on a web app. Exploits the site's trust in the user.
Accessing objects by manipulating predictable identifiers without proper authorization check.
Attacker makes the server fetch attacker-specified URLs — can reach internal services.
Primary mitigation for CSRF — unique unpredictable token per session that attacker cannot forge.

matching.prompt: Using ../ to navigate outside web root
Tricks authenticated user into submitting unwanted request
Changing ?id=123 to ?id=124 to access another user's data
Making server fetch attacker-controlled URLs
Primary mitigation for CSRF

questions.q: A directory traversal attack uses:
opts: SQL code injected into file paths
'../' sequences in URLs to navigate outside the intended web root directory and access sensitive system files
JavaScript to modify file paths
DNS queries to locate files
questions.exp: Directory traversal uses ../ to move up directory levels — potentially reaching /etc/passwd or other sensitive files.

questions.q: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks exploit:
opts: The attacker's trust in the server
The server's trust in the authenticated victim's browser — tricking authenticated users into making unwanted requests
SQL injection vulnerabilities
DNS poisoning
questions.exp: CSRF exploits that web apps trust requests from authenticated sessions — the attacker tricks the victim's browser into sending malicious requests.

questions.q: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerabilities allow attackers to:
opts: Inject SQL into databases
Access objects belonging to other users by manipulating predictable identifiers without proper authorization checks
Execute code on the server
Bypass network firewalls
questions.exp: IDOR occurs when apps expose internal object IDs — changing ?account=1001 to ?account=1002 might access another user's data.

questions.q: The primary mitigation for CSRF attacks is:
opts: Input validation
Content Security Policy
Unique, unpredictable CSRF tokens per session that must be included in state-changing requests
HTTPS encryption
questions.exp: CSRF tokens are secret, unique values the attacker cannot guess or forge — requests without valid tokens are rejected.

scenarios.q: A web application shows account statements via /statements?account=10452. A researcher changes the parameter to 10453 and receives another customer's full financial statement without error or authentication challenge. What vulnerability is this, and what is the fix?
opts: SQL injection — the account parameter is passed to a SQL query without sanitization

IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) — the application exposes a direct reference to internal objects without verifying the requester owns that object. Fix: verify server-side that the authenticated user owns the requested account before returning data
CSRF — the attacker forged a cross-site request
Directory traversal — the account number is used as a file path
scenarios.exp: IDOR: the application uses a predictable identifier (account number) as a direct database reference without an authorization check. Fix is server-side: before returning account 10453's data, verify the authenticated session belongs to the owner of account 10453. Client-side controls don't help — attackers modify HTTP requests directly.

scenarios.q: A developer builds a web preview feature where users provide a URL and the server fetches content to display. A researcher provides: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ and receives the application's cloud instance metadata including IAM credentials. What vulnerability is this?
opts: CSRF — the server is making requests on behalf of the user
SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) — the server fetches attacker-specified URLs, giving access to internal resources (including cloud metadata services) only accessible from the server itself
Directory traversal — the URL traverses to internal file paths
SQL injection — the URL parameter is passed to a database query
scenarios.exp: SSRF: the cloud metadata service at 169.254.169.254 is accessible only from within the cloud instance — not from the internet. But the server IS the cloud instance. By making the server fetch that URL, the attacker accesses internal resources as if they were the server. Fix: validate/restrict URLs the server will fetch, block private IP ranges.

scenarios.q: Authenticated users can submit support tickets. A researcher discovers they can embed JavaScript in a ticket that executes when a support agent views it in the admin console. The researcher uses this to capture the admin's session cookie. What type of XSS is this, and why is it particularly dangerous?
opts: Reflected XSS — dangerous because it affects all users who click a crafted link

Stored XSS — particularly dangerous because it targets privileged admin accounts. A support agent viewing the ticket executes the attacker's script with admin session privileges, enabling account takeover of a high-privilege user
DOM-based XSS — dangerous because it bypasses server-side filtering
CSRF — the ticket submission forges requests on the admin's behalf
scenarios.exp: Stored XSS targeting admins: the payload is stored in the ticket and executes when a privileged user views it. This is privilege escalation via XSS — an unprivileged user executing code in a privileged user's browser context. This is how many high-profile compromises begin.

cram.def: Exploits probability that two different inputs produce the same hash (collision). Easier than brute force at large scale.
Forces communication to use a weaker, older, vulnerable protocol version.
Padding Oracle On Downgraded Legacy Encryption — exploits SSLv3 via protocol downgrade.
Precomputed hash-to-plaintext database. Defeated by salting.
bcrypt, PBKDF2, Argon2 — deliberately slow hashing algorithms making brute force impractical.

matching.prompt: Two different inputs producing the same hash
Forcing use of weaker protocol version
SSLv3 padding oracle exploitation
Precomputed hash database — defeated by salts
Deliberately slow hashing to resist brute force

questions.q: A birthday attack exploits the fact that:
opts: Users tend to use birthdays as passwords
Finding two inputs that produce the same hash is much easier than brute-forcing a specific hash
Birthday data is stored in databases
Attackers can guess birthdays from social media
questions.exp: The birthday problem shows that hash collisions occur far sooner than intuition suggests — exploited to break MD5 and SHA-1.

questions.q: A downgrade attack forces a connection to:
opts: Use a newer, untested protocol
Use a weaker, older, vulnerable protocol version that the attacker can then exploit
Disconnect entirely
Use stronger encryption
questions.exp: Downgrade attacks exploit backward compatibility — forcing use of deprecated protocols like SSLv3 that have known vulnerabilities.

questions.q: Key stretching algorithms like bcrypt mitigate brute force attacks by:
opts: Encrypting the password before hashing
Using asymmetric keys for password storage
Being deliberately computationally slow — making large-scale password cracking impractical
Requiring a hardware token
questions.exp: Key stretching applies many iterations of hashing — taking milliseconds per attempt is fine for login but makes billions of guesses per second impractical.

scenarios.q: An organization uses MD5 to verify software download integrity. A researcher demonstrates they can create a malicious file with the same MD5 hash as the legitimate file. Users download and verify the hash — it matches — and install the malware. What cryptographic attack made this possible?
opts: A brute-force attack against MD5 — use longer MD5 hashes
A hash collision attack — MD5 has known collision vulnerabilities allowing two different inputs to produce the same hash. Use SHA-256 or SHA-3 which are collision-resistant
A rainbow table attack against MD5 — salt the download hashes
A downgrade attack forcing MD5 instead of SHA-256
scenarios.exp: Collision attack on MD5: it's computationally feasible to find two different inputs with the same MD5 hash. MD5 was broken for this purpose in 2004. SHA-256 (and SHA-3) are collision-resistant. For software integrity verification, SHA-256 is the minimum.

scenarios.q: A TLS connection between a client and server negotiates to TLS 1.0 with RC4 cipher despite both supporting TLS 1.3. An attacker on the network modified the ClientHello, altering the supported cipher suites list before forwarding it to the server. What attack is this and how should the server prevent it?
opts: An SSL stripping attack — prevent by enabling HSTS

A downgrade attack — the server should be configured to reject connections using TLS versions below 1.2, refusing to negotiate with the modified ClientHello regardless of what the client claims to support
A replay attack on the TLS handshake
A birthday attack on the RC4 stream cipher
scenarios.exp: Protocol downgrade attack: the attacker manipulates negotiation to force use of a weaker protocol the server still supports. Fix: remove TLS 1.0 and 1.1 from the server's supported versions entirely — if the server won't negotiate TLS 1.0, the downgrade can't succeed. Also remove RC4 and other weak ciphers.

cram.def: Try ONE common password against MANY accounts — avoids lockout. E.g. 'Password1' on all accounts.
Using stolen username/password pairs from one breach to try on other services (exploits password reuse).
Using a captured password hash directly for authentication — no need to crack it.
Dictionary words + rule modifications (capitalize, add numbers/symbols).
Locks account after N failed attempts — mitigates brute force but not password spraying.

matching.prompt: One common password tried against many accounts
Stolen credentials tried on other services
Using captured hash directly for authentication
Why password spraying evades account lockout
matching.answer: One guess per account — never triggers lockout threshold

questions.q: Password spraying differs from traditional brute force in that:
opts: Password spraying uses encrypted passwords
Password spraying tries one common password against many accounts to avoid triggering account lockout
Password spraying requires physical access
Password spraying always succeeds
questions.exp: Spraying goes wide, not deep — one guess per account keeps it below lockout thresholds while targeting many accounts.

questions.q: Credential stuffing attacks work because:
opts: Attackers guess common passwords
Many users reuse the same username and password across multiple services — breached credentials from one site work on others
Attackers have access to password databases
Stolen hashes are cracked before use
questions.exp: Credential stuffing exploits password reuse — a breach at Site A gives attackers credentials that work on Site B.

questions.q: Which attack is MOST effectively mitigated by MFA but NOT account lockout?
opts: Brute force
Password spraying
Credential stuffing
Pass-the-hash
questions.exp: Credential stuffing uses valid credentials — account lockout won't trigger. MFA prevents access even with correct credentials.

questions.q: Pass-the-hash is particularly dangerous because:
opts: It cracks passwords instantly
The attacker doesn't need to know the actual password — the hash itself is the authentication credential that can be replayed
It only works on Linux systems
It requires the attacker to physically access the system
questions.exp: NTLM authentication accepts the hash directly — attackers capture hashes from memory and use them without cracking.

scenarios.q: An attacker obtains 10 million leaked username/password pairs from a social media breach. A script tests these credentials against a financial institution's login page. 23,000 pairs succeed — users who reused their social media password for their bank account. What attack is this, and what control would have been MOST effective?
opts: Brute force attack — prevented by account lockout after 5 failed attempts
Credential stuffing — most effectively prevented by MFA, which makes the correct password insufficient on its own
Dictionary attack — prevented by requiring complex passwords
Password spraying — prevented by monitoring for single passwords across multiple accounts
scenarios.exp: Credential stuffing exploits password reuse — it uses correct passwords, so account lockout and complexity requirements don't help. MFA is the most effective control: even with the correct password, the attacker needs the second factor they don't have.

scenarios.q: A SOC team notices in their authentication logs: between 9-11am each day, exactly one failed login attempt occurs against each of 3,000 user accounts, using the password 'Welcome1'. No accounts are locked out. What attack is occurring and why does it evade traditional brute-force detection?
opts: A brute-force attack — it's just particularly slow

Password spraying — it deliberately stays below account lockout thresholds (one attempt per account per day) while testing a common password against many accounts. Lockout policies count failed attempts PER ACCOUNT, not per password
Credential stuffing — the attacker has a database of valid credentials
Dictionary attack — the attacker is working through a password list one entry at a time
scenarios.exp: Password spraying: lockout policies count failures per account. Spraying makes only ONE attempt per account — never triggering per-account lockout. Detection requires correlation across accounts (one password, many accounts). Mitigations: MFA, block commonly-used passwords like 'Welcome1', anomaly detection on authentication patterns.

cram.def: Login from two distant cities minutes apart — account likely compromised (credential theft).
Unexpected CPU/GPU/bandwidth spike — possible cryptominer or data exfiltration.
Large data transfers to unusual destinations — possible data exfiltration or C2 communication.
MD5/SHA-256 hash of known malware. Exact match detection. Easily evaded by polymorphic malware.
Known malicious IP addresses, domains, URLs. Used in firewall rules and DNS sinkholes.
Gaps in logs, log deletion, unusually large logs — sign of cover-up or active attack.

matching.prompt: Login from two distant cities minutes apart
Unexpected CPU spike on a server
Large data transfer to unknown external IP
Gaps or deletions in system logs
Known malicious file hash
Known malicious IP address or domain

questions.q: An impossible travel alert fires when:
opts: A user logs in multiple times in one day
A user account shows logins from geographically impossible locations within a short time — indicating credential compromise
A server runs out of disk space
A user downloads a large file
questions.exp: Impossible travel detects when the same account logs in from locations too far apart for human travel — strong indicator of credential theft.

questions.q: Unexpected high CPU usage on a server with no scheduled processes is an indicator of:
opts: Normal background maintenance
Possible cryptomining malware or other resource-intensive malicious process
A failed software update
Disk encryption
questions.exp: Cryptominers use victim CPU/GPU resources — unexplained high utilization is an IoC to investigate.

questions.q: File hash IoCs are easily evaded by:
opts: Firewall rules
Polymorphic malware that changes its code — and therefore its hash — with each infection
Encrypted communications
Legitimate software signatures
questions.exp: Hash-based IoCs match exact files — polymorphic malware changes its hash with each infection, evading hash-based detection.

scenarios.q: A SIEM alert fires: 'User jsmith logged in from London at 09:00 and from Singapore at 09:45.' The user's manager confirms jsmith is in the London office today. What does this IoC indicate, and what are the IMMEDIATE response actions?
opts: A misconfigured SIEM rule generating false positives — tune the rule and close the alert

An impossible travel alert strongly indicating credential compromise. Immediate actions: disable jsmith's account, force session termination on all active sessions, require credential reset via verified out-of-band channel, check what was accessed from the Singapore session
Standard VPN usage — the Singapore IP is likely a VPN endpoint
An NTP synchronization error causing timestamp confusion
scenarios.exp: Impossible travel is a strong credential compromise indicator. Immediate actions matter: disable the account first (stop active damage), terminate all sessions, then investigate. The out-of-band channel for credential reset is important — if email is compromised, emailing a reset link helps the attacker.

scenarios.q: A threat hunter reviews DNS logs and notices an internal workstation making periodic DNS queries every 60 seconds to a domain registered 3 days ago that has no web presence. The workstation's user has not reported any issues. What does this pattern suggest?
opts: Normal software update checking behavior — no action needed

Beaconing — malware periodically checking in with C2 infrastructure using a recently registered domain (a common evasion technique). Isolate the workstation, collect memory dump, review all processes and network connections, trace what was executed in the past 3 days
A DNS tunneling attack exfiltrating data in the queries themselves
A botnet scanning behavior
scenarios.exp: Beaconing IoC: regular interval (60 seconds = timer-based), newly registered domain (threat feeds don't have it yet), no legitimate purpose. This is characteristic malware C2 check-in behavior. The user reporting no issues is expected — modern malware is designed to be silent. Memory forensics will reveal the malicious process.

cram.def: Dividing network into isolated segments. Limits lateral movement if one segment is compromised.
Virtual LAN — logical segmentation at Layer 2. Isolates traffic without separate physical hardware.
Isolated network segment for public-facing servers. Between internet and internal network.
Complete physical isolation — no network connection. Used for most sensitive systems.
Only approved applications can run. Default-deny. Strongest application control.
Granular segmentation — each workload isolated. Core Zero Trust architecture component.

matching.prompt: Isolated network for public-facing servers
Logical isolation at Layer 2
Complete physical network isolation
Only approved apps can run
Per-workload isolation in Zero Trust

questions.q: The primary security benefit of network segmentation is:
opts: Improved network speed
Limiting lateral movement — if one segment is compromised, attackers cannot easily reach other segments
Reduced hardware costs
Simplified network management
questions.exp: Segmentation contains breaches — compromising one VLAN or segment doesn't automatically give access to the rest of the network.

questions.q: A DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) is used to:
opts: Encrypt all internal traffic
Host public-facing servers in an isolated segment between the internet and internal network
Store backups securely
Monitor network traffic
questions.exp: A DMZ exposes public services (web, email) to the internet while protecting the internal network if those services are compromised.

questions.q: An air gap provides the STRONGEST isolation because:
opts: It uses the strongest encryption
The system has absolutely no network connection — it cannot be remotely attacked
It uses multiple firewalls
It requires biometric authentication
questions.exp: An air-gapped system has no network connectivity — remote attacks are impossible. Used for the most critical systems.

questions.q: An application allow list is more effective than a block list because:
opts: It requires less maintenance
It blocks everything by default and only permits explicitly approved applications — a much stronger default-deny posture
It is faster to configure
It doesn't require administrator rights
questions.exp: Allow listing is default-deny — only approved apps run. Block listing is default-allow — you must know every bad app to block it.

scenarios.q: A manufacturing company's flat network has all 500 workstations, 20 servers, 15 production line controllers, and corporate WiFi on the same subnet with no internal firewall controls. Ransomware on one workstation encrypts 340 workstations, all 20 servers, and 11 production controllers before being contained. What architectural change would have MOST limited the ransomware's lateral movement?
opts: Deploying antivirus on all workstations
Implementing network segmentation with VLANs and internal firewall rules isolating workstations, servers, production controllers, and WiFi into separate zones
Enabling host-based firewalls on all workstations
Deploying an IDS to detect the ransomware spreading
scenarios.exp: Network segmentation is the core answer. In a flat network, ransomware can reach everything. Segmentation with zone-based firewall rules means workstations can't reach production controllers directly. The blast radius is contained to the segment where infection occurs. The production controller infection was the most serious — OT network segmentation is critical.

scenarios.q: An organization deploys application allow-listing permitting only signed binaries from approved vendors. A red team bypasses this by using mshta.exe (a legitimate, signed Microsoft binary) to execute a malicious HTA file hosted on a remote server. The allow-list is not triggered. What technique bypassed the control, and what additional detection would catch it?
opts: The red team exploited a buffer overflow in the allow-listing software

Living-off-the-land (LOLBins) — mshta.exe is a whitelisted signed binary. The allow-list permits it, but the malicious behavior is what it's doing. Detection: EDR with behavioral rules flagging mshta.exe making external network connections
The red team used an unsigned binary matching a known-good file hash
The allow-list was misconfigured to allow all Microsoft signed binaries
scenarios.exp: LOLBins bypass allow-listing by definition — the binary IS allowed. The control works at process creation; it can't control what the allowed process does. Behavioral monitoring catches this: mshta.exe calling out to the internet is anomalous. EDR rules on LOLBin behavior are a necessary complement to allow-listing.

cram.def: Applying vendor-released fixes for known vulnerabilities. Most important ongoing security activity.
Users and processes get only minimum access needed. Limits damage from compromise.
Quarantining compromised or suspicious systems to prevent spread.
Properly retiring and securely wiping end-of-life systems to prevent data exposure.
Automated tools ensure systems maintain secure baseline configurations. Prevents configuration drift.

matching.prompt: Most important ongoing security activity
Users get only minimum necessary access
Quarantining a compromised system
Securely retiring end-of-life systems
Automated enforcement of secure configurations

questions.q: Which mitigation technique MOST directly reduces the risk from known vulnerabilities?
opts: Encryption
Network segmentation
Patching — applying vendor-released security fixes
MFA
questions.exp: Patching removes the vulnerability — other controls reduce impact but don't fix the underlying flaw.

questions.q: Least privilege as a mitigation technique means:
opts: Removing all user accounts except administrators
Granting users and processes only the minimum access necessary to perform their functions — nothing more
Requiring MFA for all access
Encrypting all user data
questions.exp: Least privilege limits the blast radius of a compromise — an attacker who compromises a limited account has limited access.

questions.q: Isolating a compromised system is done to:
opts: Preserve the system for forensic analysis only
Prevent the spread of malware or attacker access to other systems while maintaining the compromised system for investigation
Delete all malware immediately
Restore the system from backup
questions.exp: Isolation quarantines the infected system — stopping spread while preserving evidence for forensic investigation.

scenarios.q: A security team patches a critical RCE vulnerability in their web application framework within 4 hours of vendor announcement. One week later, they discover their systems were compromised — the attacker exploited the same vulnerability 6 hours before the patch was released. The patch was deployed in time but the system was already compromised. What does this demonstrate?
opts: Patching is ineffective and shouldn't be prioritized

Patching is the most important control for known vulnerabilities, but it cannot protect against exploitation that occurred before the patch existed. Defense-in-depth controls already in place — network segmentation, least privilege, behavioral monitoring — limit damage from zero-day exploitation
The team should have patched faster — 4 hours is too slow
The vendor should have released the patch before the vulnerability was public
scenarios.exp: Patching is essential but not sufficient. Zero-day attacks occur before patches are available. Existing controls — network segmentation, least privilege, behavioral monitoring — determine how much damage a successful pre-patch exploit causes. Patch fast AND maintain layered defenses.

scenarios.q: An analyst receives an alert that a workstation is attempting to connect to 47 different internal IP addresses on port 445 (SMB) within 5 minutes. The workstation belongs to an accountant with no IT duties. The analyst isolates the workstation. What is the NEXT step?
opts: The analyst applied least privilege by revoking the accountant's network permissions

The analyst applied isolation as a containment technique. Next step: preserve forensic evidence (memory dump before shutdown, network connection state) and begin investigation — what executed, was the user socially engineered, are the 47 IPs scanned already compromised?
The analyst applied network segmentation by moving the workstation to a guest VLAN
The analyst applied zero trust by requiring re-authentication
scenarios.exp: Isolation stops the spread (the SMB scanning is consistent with worm behavior or ransomware propagation). But isolation before forensics destroys evidence — the memory dump captures running malware and encryption keys that disappear on shutdown. Then investigate: the 47 IPs scanned may already be compromised.

cram.def: Process of securing a system by reducing its attack surface — removing unnecessary features, services, and access.
First hardening step on any new device — default passwords are publicly known.
UEFI feature verifying boot software is signed and trusted — prevents bootkit attacks.
Prescriptive hardening guides for specific OS and application versions — widely used as baselines.
Advanced endpoint security — detects malicious behavior, provides visibility and response capability.
Systematic process for applying patches promptly and consistently across the environment.

matching.prompt: First hardening step on any new device
Verifying boot software is signed and trusted
Prescriptive OS hardening guides
Advanced endpoint detection and response
Removing unused applications

questions.q: System hardening is BEST described as:
opts: Installing the latest malware protection
Reducing a system's attack surface by removing unnecessary features, services, accounts, and access
Encrypting all system data
Monitoring system logs for anomalies
questions.exp: Hardening systematically removes anything not needed for the system's intended function — reducing the ways an attacker can exploit it.

questions.q: The FIRST step when hardening a newly deployed network device should be:
opts: Enable all available features
Install the latest firmware
Change all default credentials
Configure network segmentation
questions.exp: Default credentials are publicly known — changing them immediately is the most critical first step in device hardening.

questions.q: Secure Boot mitigates which type of attack?
opts: SQL injection
Password spraying
Bootkit/rootkit attacks that modify the boot process before the OS loads
DDoS attacks
questions.exp: Secure Boot verifies the cryptographic signature of bootloader and OS — preventing unsigned malicious code from running at boot.

questions.q: EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) differs from traditional antivirus in that:
opts: EDR only detects known malware signatures
EDR provides behavioral detection, continuous monitoring, and active response capabilities — not just signature matching
EDR is only for servers
EDR requires no maintenance
questions.exp: EDR uses behavioral analysis and machine learning — detecting novel threats that bypass signature-based AV.

scenarios.q: A new Linux web server is deployed running as root, with all Apache modules enabled, PHP display_errors=On, SSH with password authentication enabled, and the default Apache welcome page visible. A security engineer is asked to harden it before production. What are the HIGHEST PRIORITY hardening actions?
opts: Install antivirus and enable the host firewall

Run the web server process as a non-root dedicated user (privilege containment), disable unused Apache modules (attack surface reduction), disable display_errors in PHP (information disclosure), and disable password SSH authentication in favor of key-based auth
Change the hostname and SSH port to obscure the server's identity
Enable SELinux and configure all policies from scratch
scenarios.exp: Hardening priority: run as non-root (if exploited, attacker gets web server privileges, not root), disable unused modules (each module is an attack surface), display_errors reveals code paths to attackers, password SSH is vulnerable to brute force. These are quick wins with highest impact.

scenarios.q: An organization implements Secure Boot on all new laptops. Six months later, a red team boots a laptop from a USB drive containing a customized OS that bypasses all endpoint controls. Investigation reveals Secure Boot is enabled but the UEFI settings have no password and allow USB to boot first. What failed?
opts: Secure Boot doesn't protect against USB boot attacks

Secure Boot was configured correctly but the surrounding hardening was incomplete — without a UEFI firmware password and boot order restriction, an attacker can simply disable Secure Boot or reorder to boot USB first. Complete hardening requires: Secure Boot + UEFI password + boot order locked to internal drive only
The UEFI firmware has a vulnerability that bypasses Secure Boot
The USB drive had a Microsoft-signed bootloader that Secure Boot accepted
scenarios.exp: Layered hardening: an attacker with physical access and no UEFI password can disable Secure Boot or change boot order in 30 seconds. The complete hardening: Secure Boot (verified boot chain) + UEFI password (prevents configuration changes) + boot order locked (prevents USB boot) + full disk encryption (protects data even if they succeed).

cram.def: Provider supplies virtualized compute, storage, networking. Customer manages OS upward. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs.
Provider manages infrastructure + OS + runtime. Customer manages application and data. Examples: Heroku.
Provider manages everything. Customer just uses the application. Examples: Microsoft 365, Salesforce.
IaaS: customer responsible for most. SaaS: provider responsible for most. Security always shared.
Defining and provisioning infrastructure via code (Terraform, CloudFormation). Enables repeatable, auditable deployments.
No server management — code runs in response to events. Provider handles all infrastructure. Examples: AWS Lambda.
Security policy enforcement point between users and cloud services — visibility, compliance, data security.
Combination of public and private cloud — workloads move between environments.

matching.prompt: Provider supplies VMs; customer manages OS upward
Provider manages everything; customer uses app
Defining infrastructure via code files
Code runs on events; no server management
Security enforcement between users and cloud services
Mixed public and private cloud

questions.q: In the IaaS cloud model, the customer is responsible for:
opts: Physical hardware security
The operating system, applications, and data — the provider manages physical infrastructure and virtualization
Only the application data
Nothing — the provider handles all security
questions.exp: IaaS provides raw compute — the customer manages everything from the OS upward including patching, hardening, and data.

questions.q: Which cloud service model provides the LEAST customer control over the underlying infrastructure?
opts: IaaS
PaaS
SaaS
On-premises
questions.exp: SaaS gives customers the least control — the provider manages everything including infrastructure, OS, runtime, and application.

questions.q: A CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) is used to:
opts: Provide physical security for cloud data centers
Enforce security policies between users and cloud services — providing visibility, compliance, and data security
Replace firewalls in cloud environments
Manage cloud billing and costs
questions.exp: CASBs sit between users and cloud services — enforcing DLP, access control, encryption, and compliance policies.

questions.q: A hybrid cloud architecture introduces which unique security challenge?
opts: Lack of encryption support
Securing data and consistent policy enforcement across both private and public cloud environments
No access control options
Physical security of data centers
questions.exp: Hybrid clouds require consistent security policies, data classification, and access controls across two different environments.

scenarios.q: A startup is building a new SaaS application. The CTO wants to deploy on AWS and has asked the security engineer to outline who is responsible for what. The engineer draws the line at: AWS is responsible for the physical data centers, networking hardware, and hypervisor; the startup is responsible for the operating system, application code, data, and IAM configuration. Which cloud service model does this describe?
opts: SaaS — the provider manages everything including the application
PaaS — the provider manages the OS and runtime, the startup manages the application
IaaS — the provider manages infrastructure up to and including the hypervisor; the customer manages everything above
On-premises — no cloud provider is involved
scenarios.exp: IaaS shared responsibility: the cloud provider secures the physical infrastructure and virtualization layer. The customer is responsible for everything from the OS upward — OS patching, application security, data protection, and IAM configuration. This is the most customer-managed cloud model.

scenarios.q: A company migrates its entire application stack to AWS and assumes that because AWS is 'secure', they don't need to configure security groups, encrypt their S3 buckets, or audit their IAM roles. Three months later, they suffer a data breach via an exposed S3 bucket. Which fundamental concept did the company fail to understand?
opts: AWS has a bug in their S3 security controls
The shared responsibility model — AWS secures the infrastructure, but the customer is responsible for what they put on it, including data classification, access control, and encryption configuration
Cloud providers are always less secure than on-premises
AWS should have blocked the public S3 access automatically without customer configuration
scenarios.exp: Shared responsibility model failure: AWS secures the platform; the customer secures their configuration and data. S3 bucket ACLs, encryption at rest, and IAM policies are always the customer's responsibility regardless of cloud model. 'AWS is secure' doesn't mean 'my deployment on AWS is secure'.

scenarios.q: An organization's development team deploys Lambda functions for a serverless application. The security team reviews the setup and finds: each Lambda function has an IAM role with AdministratorAccess, environment variables contain database credentials in plaintext, and error messages returned to users include stack traces. Which cloud security concern is MOST immediately critical?
opts: The serverless architecture itself is the vulnerability — migrate to EC2

The IAM roles with AdministratorAccess are the most critical — if any Lambda function is compromised, the attacker gains full AWS account control. Remediate by applying least privilege to each function's specific permissions
The plaintext credentials in environment variables are critical and should use AWS Secrets Manager instead
The stack traces in error messages reveal code structure to attackers and should be disabled
scenarios.exp: Overprivileged IAM roles are the most critical finding: a compromised Lambda function with AdministratorAccess gives the attacker complete control of the AWS account — all services, all data, all regions. Plaintext credentials and stack traces are serious but limited in scope. Least privilege on IAM is the foundational cloud security control.

scenarios.q: A financial services firm uses a SaaS CRM system to store customer contact information and interaction history. The firm's CISO asks the security team to document who is responsible for encrypting the data at rest within the CRM database. What is the correct answer under the shared responsibility model?
opts: The financial services firm — they must deploy their own encryption layer over the SaaS application
The SaaS provider — in SaaS, the provider manages the entire stack including the database and its encryption
It depends on which cloud provider the SaaS vendor uses
There is no encryption responsibility in SaaS — the provider handles all security
scenarios.exp: SaaS shared responsibility: in SaaS, the provider manages everything — infrastructure, OS, application, and database. Database encryption at rest is the SaaS provider's responsibility. The customer's responsibility in SaaS is primarily data governance (what data they put in, who in their organization has access), not infrastructure security.

scenarios.q: A company is deciding between deploying an application using IaaS (EC2 instances they manage) or PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk where AWS manages the OS and runtime). The security team prefers PaaS. What is the BEST security justification for choosing PaaS over IaaS for this workload?
opts: PaaS is always cheaper than IaaS
In PaaS, the provider manages OS patching and runtime security — reducing the customer's attack surface and eliminating the risk of unpatched OS vulnerabilities due to missed patching cycles
PaaS prevents all application-level vulnerabilities
IaaS is inherently insecure because the customer manages too much
scenarios.exp: PaaS security benefit: the provider handles OS and runtime patching — one of the most common sources of compromise (unpatched OS vulnerabilities). The customer still owns application code and data security, but the OS attack surface is managed by the provider who has dedicated teams and automation for this purpose.

cram.def: Separates control plane from data plane. Centralized, programmable network control. Rapid policy changes.
Traffic between systems within the same network (internal). Traditional security focused on north-south only.
Traffic entering and leaving the network perimeter. Traditional firewall focus.
Granular per-workload isolation — inspects and controls east-west traffic. Key for Zero Trust.
Running network functions (firewall, IDS, load balancer) as virtual machines rather than dedicated hardware.
Software-defined WAN — uses multiple connectivity types. Centralized management.

matching.prompt: Separates control plane from data plane
Traffic between internal systems
Traffic entering/leaving network perimeter
Per-workload isolation in Zero Trust
Network functions as virtual machines

questions.q: East-west traffic security is important because:
opts: It is always encrypted
Attackers who breach the perimeter use lateral movement — traditional perimeter firewalls don't inspect internal traffic
It travels faster than north-south traffic
It never contains sensitive data
questions.exp: Traditional security focuses on north-south (perimeter) traffic. East-west inspection catches lateral movement after a breach.

questions.q: Microsegmentation addresses which limitation of traditional network security?
opts: Lack of encryption
Perimeter-only security that leaves internal east-west traffic uninspected — enabling attacker lateral movement
High cost of network hardware
Lack of user authentication
questions.exp: Microsegmentation applies granular security policies to east-west traffic — containing breaches to individual workloads.

questions.q: SDN (Software-Defined Networking) improves security management by:
opts: Eliminating the need for firewalls
Centralizing network control — enabling rapid, consistent policy deployment across the entire network
Using stronger encryption for all traffic
Replacing physical network hardware
questions.exp: SDN's centralized control plane enables rapid, programmatic security policy changes across the entire network from one place.

scenarios.q: A network architect is designing a new data center. The CEO asks why they need an internal firewall between the server VLAN and the workstation VLAN — 'aren't they both inside the network already?' What is the BEST justification for internal segmentation?
opts: Internal firewalls are required by all compliance frameworks

Internal segmentation limits lateral movement — if an attacker compromises a workstation, internal firewall rules prevent them from directly reaching the servers. The perimeter firewall only stops threats entering from outside, not threats already inside
Internal firewalls make the network faster by segmenting broadcast domains
VLANs alone provide all the security needed without internal firewalls
scenarios.exp: East-west traffic control: traditional perimeter-only security assumes everything inside is trusted. Modern attacks (ransomware, insider threats) compromise one internal system then move laterally. Internal segmentation forces traffic between zones through controls — limiting blast radius if a workstation is compromised.

scenarios.q: A company's web application servers sit on the same network segment as the internal corporate systems. During a penetration test, the tester compromises the web application server and immediately accesses internal HR systems, payroll databases, and Active Directory — all without additional exploitation. What architectural flaw does this reveal?
opts: The web application had too many vulnerabilities
The penetration tester used advanced techniques that couldn't be defended against
The web application servers are not in a DMZ — they should be in an isolated segment with strict firewall rules allowing only necessary traffic to internal systems
The internal systems had default credentials that made them easy to access
scenarios.exp: DMZ architecture flaw: public-facing web servers should be in a screened subnet (DMZ) isolated from internal systems. If a web server is compromised (expected attack surface), the attacker should face another firewall before reaching internal HR, payroll, and AD. A flat network where web servers can freely reach everything internal is a critical design failure.

scenarios.q: A security architect is implementing microsegmentation for a cloud workload environment. They define policies where each application tier (web, application, database) can only communicate with adjacent tiers on specific ports, and no lateral communication is permitted within a tier (web server to web server, for example). An attacker compromises one web server. What is the MOST significant benefit of this design?
opts: The attacker cannot see any other systems
The attacker cannot reach the database tier directly and cannot spread to other web servers — their movement is constrained to the single compromised web server's permitted communication paths
The attacker's traffic is automatically encrypted
The compromised web server is automatically isolated by the microsegmentation platform
scenarios.exp: Microsegmentation benefit: east-west controls limit lateral movement within the environment. The attacker can only communicate on paths the policy explicitly permits. Spreading to other web servers (lateral movement within tier) is blocked. Jumping directly to the database (skipping the application tier) is blocked. Each compromise is contained to that workload's permitted communication paths.

scenarios.q: A company is evaluating SD-WAN to replace their MPLS WAN. The security team has concerns. The network team argues SD-WAN actually improves security posture compared to MPLS. Which argument BEST supports the network team's position?
opts: SD-WAN is always cheaper, which frees budget for other security controls

SD-WAN enables consistent, centrally-managed security policies across all branch locations — including integrated encryption of branch traffic and the ability to route directly to cloud services with security controls applied, which MPLS backhauling to a central firewall didn't provide for cloud traffic
SD-WAN eliminates the need for branch firewalls because the SD-WAN controller handles all security
MPLS is inherently encrypted so SD-WAN is less secure
scenarios.exp: SD-WAN security advantage: centralized policy management with encryption across all WAN links, and the ability to apply security controls (FWaaS, CASB) close to the user rather than backhauling all traffic to a central data center. MPLS doesn't encrypt traffic natively. SD-WAN with SASE provides better cloud-era security than MPLS backhauling.

cram.def: Lightweight application packaging. Containers share host OS kernel. Docker, Kubernetes. Faster than VMs.
Network-connected embedded devices — cameras, HVAC, medical devices. Often lack security controls.
Industrial Control Systems. Control physical processes. Critical infrastructure. Availability top priority.
Hardware and software controlling physical equipment. Different priorities than IT — availability > confidentiality.
Processing data close to where it's generated — IoT devices, edge nodes. Reduces latency, increases attack surface.

matching.prompt: Lightweight apps sharing host OS kernel
Network-connected embedded devices
Control physical industrial processes
IT that controls physical equipment
Processing data near where it is generated

questions.q: IoT devices present unique security challenges because:
opts: They are always connected via encrypted protocols
They often lack adequate security controls, have long lifecycles, are difficult to patch, and have limited computational resources
They are always behind corporate firewalls
They only connect to internal networks
questions.exp: IoT devices ship with minimal security, default credentials, and rare firmware updates — making them persistent vulnerabilities.

questions.q: ICS/SCADA systems have different security priorities than traditional IT because:
opts: They use stronger encryption
Availability is the top priority — downtime in industrial control systems can have physical, safety, or economic consequences
They are never connected to networks
They only process non-sensitive data
questions.exp: In OT/ICS, a patch that requires downtime may be unacceptable — availability and safety often outweigh confidentiality concerns.

questions.q: Containerization differs from full virtualization in that:
opts: Containers are always more secure than VMs
Containers share the host OS kernel — making them more lightweight but providing less isolation than separate VMs
Containers cannot be networked together
Containers always run on physical hardware
questions.exp: Containers share the host kernel — a kernel vulnerability can affect all containers on the host. VMs have separate kernels for stronger isolation.

scenarios.q: A hospital's biomedical engineering team connects patient monitoring devices, infusion pumps, and imaging systems to the same network as clinical workstations and the corporate email system. The IT security team flags this as a critical risk. The biomedical team says the devices have never been hacked. What is the BEST explanation of the risk?
opts: Medical devices are too old to be hacked — the risk is overestimated

Medical devices typically run embedded OSes that cannot be patched, may have default credentials, and are designed for availability not security — placing them on the same network as corporate systems means a malware infection on one segment can reach life-critical devices, or a compromised medical device can be used to reach corporate systems
The risk only applies if the devices are connected to the internet directly
The biomedical team is correct — dedicated medical devices are secure by design
scenarios.exp: OT/medical device risk: these devices often run unpatched embedded OSes, have hardcoded credentials, and can't have security software installed. They must be available at all times (patient safety). Placing them on the same network as corporate systems creates a bidirectional risk: malware from corporate reaches medical, or a compromised medical device becomes a pivot to corporate. Network segmentation is the required mitigation.

scenarios.q: A manufacturing company connects its production line PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers) to the corporate network to enable remote monitoring by management. Six months later, a ransomware infection that entered via a phishing email encrypts several PLC configuration files, halting production for three days. What architectural principle was violated?
opts: The PLCs should have had antivirus installed
