Replace all 15 security tools with a single platform
scenarios.exp: People capacity through MSSP + automation: three analysts cannot sustainably cover 24/7 monitoring, incident response, and strategic security. MSSPs provide 24/7 SOC coverage with economies of scale; the internal team retains ownership of incidents, policies, and strategic decisions requiring internal knowledge. SOAR reduces mechanical workload for both the MSSP and internal team. This is a realistic capacity solution for a growing company.

scenarios.q: A company plans to migrate from on-premises infrastructure to cloud. The security architect notes that the migration will require the existing security team to develop new skills in cloud security, IAM, and CSPM tools, while simultaneously maintaining security operations for the on-premises systems during the transition. What capacity planning risk does this represent?
opts: No risk — cloud security is simpler than on-premises security

A skills and bandwidth capacity risk: the team must run two parallel operational environments (on-premises and cloud) during transition while upskilling in cloud-specific security disciplines. Without additional capacity (temporary contractors, MSSP coverage for routine monitoring, or a phased migration that doesn't overwhelm the team), security posture degrades in one or both environments during the transition period
Cloud migrations automatically improve security posture so the risk is overstated
The risk only applies to large enterprises, not mid-sized companies
scenarios.exp: Migration capacity risk: the 'two-speed operation' problem — maintaining existing systems while building new capability simultaneously. Security teams that are stretched thin during migrations make mistakes in both environments. Planning options: phased migration to reduce parallel operation duration, temporary contractor support for specific cloud skills, or MSSP coverage to free internal analyst time for upskilling.

cram.def: Discussion-based DR simulation — team talks through response to a scenario. No actual systems involved.
Recovery systems brought online alongside production — both running simultaneously. Validates recovery without risk.
Production actually fails over to recovery — real outage. Most realistic, highest risk and cost.
Practicing specific DR scenarios — more realistic than tabletop but less disruptive than full test.
After any test or real incident — document what worked, what didn't, and update plans accordingly.

matching.prompt: Discussion-based scenario walkthrough
Recovery systems brought up alongside production
Production actually fails over — highest risk
Post-incident review of what worked/failed

questions.q: A tabletop exercise differs from a full interruption test in that:
opts: Tabletop tests use real systems
A tabletop exercise is discussion-based — no actual systems are involved or tested
Full interruption tests are cheaper
They test different scenarios
questions.exp: Tabletop = talk through the plan. Full interruption = actually fail over production. Tabletop has no real-world impact.

questions.q: A full interruption test is the MOST realistic DR test because:
opts: It uses the most complex scenarios
Production actually fails over to recovery systems — revealing real-world issues that tabletop or parallel tests miss
It involves the most people
It tests only network connectivity
questions.exp: Full interruption tests actual failover of production — the highest fidelity but also the highest risk and cost.

questions.q: DR plans should be tested:
opts: Only after a real disaster occurs
Never — testing risks causing an outage
Regularly (at least annually) and after any significant infrastructure change
Only when the board requests it
questions.exp: Untested DR plans often fail when needed — regular testing finds gaps before a real disaster. Annual minimum; test after major changes.

scenarios.q: A company conducts an annual disaster recovery tabletop exercise. The exercise scenario is: 'The primary data center has suffered a flood and is completely unavailable.' The IT director walks through the DR plan step by step with the team. After the exercise, the team reports the plan 'seems solid.' Six months later, a real disaster occurs and recovery takes 3 days instead of the planned 4 hours. What did the tabletop exercise fail to reveal?
opts: Tabletop exercises are always sufficient — the 3-day recovery was due to unforeseeable complications

Tabletop exercises test the plan on paper but don't reveal practical execution failures: systems that no longer replicate correctly, backup restoration procedures that fail in practice, personnel who've left or changed roles since the plan was written, third-party dependencies that don't respond as expected, and network configuration differences at the DR site. A parallel test or full interruption test would have revealed these gaps before the real disaster
The tabletop exercise was conducted incorrectly
The IT director should not have led the exercise
scenarios.exp: Tabletop vs operational testing: tabletop exercises reveal plan logic gaps and communication issues. They don't reveal whether the technical recovery actually works. The only way to know if backups restore correctly in the expected time is to restore them. The only way to know if the DR site comes up correctly is to bring it up. Annual tabletop only, with no actual recovery test, is a false sense of assurance.

scenarios.q: An organization conducts a parallel DR test — they bring up their recovery site while production remains running. The test is declared successful because all systems came online at the DR site. Three months later during a real failover, they discover their critical Oracle database had a 6-hour data lag because the replication was misconfigured after a DR test cleanup. What does this reveal about their parallel testing process?
opts: Parallel tests are less valuable than tabletop exercises

The parallel test revealed systems could come up at the DR site but didn't validate data currency — a critical gap. Successful system startup is not the same as successful data replication. The test should have included: validating replication lag metrics, checking data integrity at the DR site against production, and verifying the cleanup/restoration procedure after the test returns the environment to production state
Replication failures are undetectable until a real disaster
The Oracle database should have been excluded from DR planning
scenarios.exp: Test scope vs test completeness: bringing systems online is necessary but not sufficient. Data currency, replication health, and post-test environment hygiene are equally important to validate. A DR test should explicitly verify: (1) systems boot, (2) data is current to within RPO, (3) applications function with DR data, (4) the test cleanup restores production replication correctly. Each of these can fail independently.

scenarios.q: A financial services firm's DR plan has never been tested with a full interruption test because management is concerned about the risk of a test-induced outage. The CISO argues an untested DR plan is more dangerous than the risk of a test. How should the CISO frame this argument?
opts: The CISO is wrong — testing DR plans always causes outages that justify never testing

A DR plan that has never been tested provides false assurance — it may contain errors, outdated information, or technical failures that are only discovered during an actual disaster when the stakes are highest. The risk of a planned, controlled full interruption test (with rollback procedures, done during a low-traffic window) is calculable and manageable. The risk of discovering the plan doesn't work during an actual disaster is catastrophic and uncontrolled. Untested DR is effectively no DR
Full interruption tests should be done daily to ensure readiness
The CISO should be overruled by operations management on this decision
scenarios.exp: Testing risk vs untested DR risk: a controlled test has known risks with prepared mitigations (rollback plan, test window, staff on standby). A real disaster with an untested plan has unknown risks at the worst possible time. The CISO's framing: 'we're choosing between a small, known risk now and a large, unknown risk when we can least afford it.' Most boards understand this once framed in business continuity terms.

cram.def: Complete copy of all data. Slowest to create, fastest to restore. Baseline for all other backup types.
Only data changed since LAST BACKUP (full or incremental). Fastest to create, slowest to restore.
All data changed since LAST FULL BACKUP. Slower to create than incremental, faster to restore.
3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite. Industry best practice.
Backup not connected to network — ransomware cannot reach it. Essential for ransomware resilience.
Regularly verify backups are complete and can be restored. An untested backup is not a backup.

matching.prompt: All data changed since last full backup
All data changed since last backup of any type
Complete copy of all data
3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite
Not connected to network — ransomware-proof

questions.q: Which backup type backs up only data changed since the LAST FULL backup?
opts: Incremental
Differential
Full
Snapshot
questions.exp: Differential backs up everything changed since the last full — grows larger each day but restores with only 2 sets (full + latest differential).

questions.q: Which backup type is slowest to restore after a failure?
opts: Full backup
Differential backup
Incremental backup
Snapshot
questions.exp: Incremental restore requires the full backup plus every incremental since — the more incrementals, the longer the restore.

questions.q: The 3-2-1 backup rule states:
opts: Back up 3 times per day, to 2 locations, within 1 hour
Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with at least 1 copy stored offsite
Use 3 backup types, 2 vendors, 1 cloud provider
Test 3 times, with 2 tools, in 1 year
questions.exp: 3-2-1: 3 copies (original + 2 backups), 2 media types (disk + tape), 1 offsite (protects against site disasters).

questions.q: Why are offline (air-gapped) backups essential for ransomware resilience?
opts: They are cheaper than online backups
Ransomware cannot encrypt backups it cannot reach — network-connected backups are often encrypted by ransomware
Offline backups restore faster
They comply with all regulations
questions.exp: Ransomware specifically targets backup systems — offline/air-gapped backups are the only way to guarantee recovery without paying ransom.

questions.q: An organization discovers their backup files are corrupted when they try to restore after a ransomware attack. This highlights the importance of:
opts: More frequent backups
Regularly testing backups by actually restoring them — an untested backup provides false confidence
Using a different backup software
Storing backups on faster media
questions.exp: An untested backup is not a backup — regular restore testing is essential to confirm backups are complete and recoverable.

scenarios.q: A company takes daily full backups at midnight. A ransomware attack encrypts all file servers at 4pm on Wednesday. The company restores from Tuesday midnight's backup. After restoration, the IT director declares the incident resolved. The business team objects. What data loss has occurred, and was the RPO met?
opts: No data loss — the backup was from last night

Approximately 16 hours of data loss: files created or modified between Tuesday midnight and Wednesday 4pm are not in the restored backup. If the company's RPO is 24 hours, the 16-hour loss is technically within RPO — but if the RPO is based on business data value (e.g., a day's worth of orders or financial transactions), the business team's objection may reflect a business RPO that's stricter than the defined IT RPO. The incident exposes a gap between IT-defined RPO and business expectations of data currency
The backup was from Tuesday, which is more than 24 hours old — the RPO was definitely breached
The company should pay the ransom to recover Wednesday's data
scenarios.exp: RPO in practice: 16 hours of lost data (midnight to 4pm) is the actual loss. Whether this breaches RPO depends on the defined RPO value. If RPO = 24 hours, it's technically met. But business-critical data created Wednesday morning (orders, customer records, financial transactions) may not be recoverable — highlighting that IT RPO and business data criticality must be aligned. The business objection suggests the RPO was never properly defined with business input.

scenarios.q: A small accounting firm backs up all client data to an external hard drive stored in the server room. The firm's office suffers a fire that destroys both the primary server and the backup drive. The owner argues they had a backup strategy. What backup principle was violated, and what was the minimum required change?
opts: No principle was violated — any backup is better than none

The 3-2-1 rule was violated: specifically the '1 copy offsite' requirement. Having backups on-site means a single site disaster (fire, flood, theft) destroys both primary data and backup simultaneously. The minimum required change: store at least one copy of backups offsite — cloud backup, a bank safety deposit box, or a partner location. The 'S' in 3-2-1 specifically exists to survive site-level disasters
The backup drive was the wrong type — tape backup would have survived the fire
Two backup drives in the server room would have been sufficient
scenarios.exp: 3-2-1 rule — the '1 offsite' requirement: the purpose of the offsite copy is specifically to survive site-level disasters. Any backup stored at the same site as primary data is vulnerable to the same physical threats (fire, flood, theft, earthquake). Offsite doesn't need to be expensive — cloud backup services cost very little for small businesses and provide site-level disaster protection that no amount of on-site redundancy can provide.

scenarios.q: A company tests their backup restoration quarterly by restoring a sample of files and verifying they're accessible. During an actual ransomware incident, they attempt a full server restore and discover the backup job had been failing silently for 3 weeks due to a storage quota exceeded error — no alerts were configured for backup failures. The most recent restorable backup is 3 weeks old. What backup management failure occurred?
opts: The backup software had a bug that should be fixed by the vendor

Two failures: (1) no monitoring or alerting on backup job failures — backup jobs must have success/failure alerting so failures are discovered immediately, not during a disaster; (2) the restore testing methodology was insufficient — restoring sample files doesn't validate complete server restore capability or catch systematic backup failures. Both failures allowed 3 weeks of silent backup degradation to go undetected
The company should have used a different backup solution
The 3-week-old backup is acceptable — the company should restore from it and accept the data loss
scenarios.exp: Backup monitoring and testing gaps: backup jobs fail silently when no alerting is configured — this is one of the most common real-world DR failures. The second gap: file-level restore testing doesn't catch systematic failures (quota exceeded, job timeout, corruption) that would affect a full restore. Comprehensive backup testing must include: (1) automated success/failure alerting, (2) full restore exercises (not just file samples), and (3) validation that restored data is consistent and complete.

scenarios.q: An organization uses continuous replication to a secondary data center as their primary DR strategy, with no traditional backup. A database administrator accidentally runs a DROP TABLE command on the production database, deleting a critical table. The command replicates immediately to the secondary data center, which was the point-in-time source for recovery. What recovery limitation does this reveal about replication-only DR?
opts: Replication is always superior to backup — this scenario is not possible

Replication replicates data AND errors — the accidental deletion propagated immediately to the replica. Without a point-in-time backup (such as database snapshots taken at regular intervals or a log-based backup allowing point-in-time recovery), the organization has no version of the table from before the deletion. Replication is excellent for site failure recovery but provides no protection against logical errors or user mistakes that propagate. Backups and replication serve different failure scenarios and both are needed
The secondary data center should have had a read-only replica
The database administrator should not have had permission to run DROP TABLE
scenarios.exp: Replication vs backup for different failure types: replication protects against hardware/site failure. Backups protect against logical corruption, accidental deletion, malware encryption, and any error that replicates. A DROP TABLE replicates immediately — replication makes it worse (both sites lose the table). Point-in-time recovery (PITR) from backup allows restoration to the moment before the drop. Both mechanisms serve essential but different DR roles.

cram.def: Battery backup providing immediate power during outages — bridges gap until generator starts. Minutes of runtime.
Provides extended power during prolonged outages. Typically diesel. Takes time to start.
UPS provides instant power; generator provides extended power. Standard data center power resilience.
Full redundancy — two complete independent power systems. If one fails entirely, the other supports all load.
Critical equipment connected to two separate power circuits — one fails, other continues.

matching.prompt: Immediate battery power during outage
Extended power for prolonged outages
Full redundancy — two complete independent power systems
Critical equipment on two separate circuits

questions.q: The primary purpose of a UPS in a data center is:
opts: Providing power for extended outages
Providing immediate battery power to bridge the gap between power failure and generator startup
Regulating voltage
Distributing power to rack equipment
questions.exp: UPS provides instant power on failure — generators take time to start. UPS bridges this gap (typically minutes).

questions.q: 2N power configuration means:
opts: Two UPS units
Two generators
A completely redundant power system — two independent power paths for every component
Power from two utility providers
questions.exp: 2N means double everything — two complete, independent power systems. If one fails entirely, the other supports all load.

questions.q: Dual power feeds for critical servers protect against:
opts: Power surges
Single power circuit failure — if one circuit fails, the server continues on the second
Generator failure
UPS battery depletion
questions.exp: Dual power feeds provide path redundancy at the equipment level — one feed fails, the other automatically takes full load.

scenarios.q: A company's data center has a single UPS providing battery backup for all servers. During a power failure, the UPS activates but fails after 4 minutes due to a faulty battery — the generator hasn't started yet. All servers crash, causing data corruption on 3 databases and a 6-hour recovery. What redundancy improvements would have prevented this outcome?
opts: Replace the UPS with a larger one — capacity was the issue

Two issues require addressing: (1) the UPS battery wasn't tested or maintained — UPS batteries degrade and must be periodically tested under load to verify actual runtime; (2) a single UPS is a single point of failure. Improvements: dual UPS systems (N+1 or 2N) so one UPS failure doesn't cause a complete power failure, regular UPS battery testing and maintenance, and ensuring the generator's startup time is within the UPS runtime guarantee
Generators should start faster — 4 minutes is too slow
The servers should have been shut down gracefully before the power failure
scenarios.exp: UPS reliability requires maintenance: batteries degrade over time — a UPS reporting 10 minutes of runtime may only deliver 4 minutes on degraded batteries. Testing under load annually (or per manufacturer recommendation) reveals actual runtime. N+1 or 2N UPS design means no single UPS failure causes a complete power event. The generator start time must be less than the verified UPS runtime — not the rated runtime on degraded batteries.

scenarios.q: A financial trading firm requires its trading systems to have zero tolerance for power interruptions — even a 100ms power fluctuation can cause trading system crashes and corrupted transaction logs. The firm is evaluating power infrastructure options. Which power architecture BEST addresses this requirement?
opts: A single large UPS with a 4-hour runtime

2N power infrastructure: two completely independent power feeds from different utility substations, two independent UPS systems (each capable of carrying full load), a generator that starts within the UPS runtime, and servers with dual power supplies connected to separate PDUs fed from separate UPS systems — any single component failure results in no power interruption because the redundant path carries full load immediately
A generator without UPS — generators are more reliable than batteries
Standard N+1 UPS configuration with a single utility feed
scenarios.exp: 2N power for zero-tolerance environments: financial trading requires uninterrupted power at every level. 2N means: dual utility feeds (different substations), dual UPS (each full capacity), dual PDUs, and servers with dual PSUs. A utility feed failure, UPS failure, or PDU failure each result in zero interruption because the fully independent parallel path takes over instantly. This is the standard for Tier IV data centers and financial trading floors.

scenarios.q: During a security assessment, an auditor finds that a hospital's data center has a UPS that hasn't been tested in 3 years, a generator that was last exercised 18 months ago (fuel tank level unknown), and no written procedure for power failure response. The data center runs systems that support patient monitoring. What is the RISK ASSESSMENT of this finding?
opts: Low risk — power failures are rare and unlikely to occur

Critical risk: (1) untested UPS may not provide expected runtime — batteries degrade silently; (2) untested generator may fail to start — fuel contamination, mechanical issues, or a dead battery on the generator starter are common after extended non-use; (3) no written procedure means staff won't know what to do during a failure. For a hospital supporting patient monitoring, a power failure with cascading UPS/generator failure is a patient safety event. Immediate remediation required: test UPS under load, test-start generator, verify fuel level and quality, document response procedures
Medium risk — the hospital has never had a power failure before
The risk is acceptable because the hospital has insurance
scenarios.exp: Power resilience risk in healthcare: untested power infrastructure in a patient-critical environment is a patient safety issue, not just an IT risk. UPS batteries fail silently. Generators fail to start if not exercised. Three years without testing means unknown actual runtime. The combination of UPS, generator, and no procedure means a power failure could cascade into total failure with no fallback — a life-safety event for patients on monitoring equipment.

cram.def: Minimum security configuration a system must meet. Starting point for all deployments.
Systems gradually diverging from secure baseline through undocumented changes.
Security Content Automation Protocol — NIST standard for automated security configuration assessment.
Prescriptive configuration guides used as security baselines.
Apply via GPO, MDM, Ansible, Puppet, Chef.

matching.prompt: Minimum security configuration all systems must meet
Systems diverging from secure baseline
NIST standard for automated config compliance checking

questions.q: A security baseline defines:
opts: The maximum security controls allowed
The minimum security configuration required for all systems in the environment
The average security level across systems
Security policies for administrators only
questions.exp: A security baseline is the minimum standard — every deployed system must meet or exceed it.

questions.q: Configuration drift is:
opts: Systems becoming more secure over time
Systems gradually diverging from their secure baseline through undocumented changes
The process of updating a baseline
Migrating from one OS to another
questions.exp: Drift occurs as changes accumulate — moving systems away from the known-good baseline.

questions.q: SCAP is used to:
opts: Conduct penetration tests
Automate security configuration assessment and compliance verification against defined standards
Monitor network traffic
Manage user identities
questions.exp: SCAP provides a standardized, automated way to check whether systems meet security configuration requirements.

scenarios.q: An organization deploys 300 new Windows workstations using a golden image. Six months later, a vulnerability scan reveals that 47 workstations have weak TLS cipher suites enabled, 23 have SMBv1 active, and 12 have the local administrator account enabled with a known default password. The security team thought the golden image hardened all of these. What most likely caused this drift?
opts: The golden image was never hardened correctly

Configuration drift — after deployment, workstations were modified by local administrators, software installs, or troubleshooting procedures that changed settings from the baseline. Group Policy enforcement was likely not configured to continuously reapply and remediate settings that drift from baseline
The vulnerability scanner produced false positives
The workstations received a Windows update that changed security settings
scenarios.exp: Configuration drift cause: hardened images start secure, but post-deployment changes accumulate. Local admin access, troubleshooting steps, and software installations change settings. Group Policy with enforcement (not just application at login) continuously remediate drift. Without continuous enforcement and monitoring (SCAP scanning, GPO enforcement), systems gradually diverge from the known-good baseline.

scenarios.q: A compliance auditor reviews a company's server baseline configuration and finds it was created 4 years ago, before a major cloud migration, and has not been updated since. The baseline still references on-premises-specific settings (local user accounts, physical NIC configurations) but says nothing about cloud security groups, IAM roles, or metadata service configuration. What is the security impact?
opts: No impact — baselines only need to be created once

The baseline is outdated and no longer reflects the current threat model or environment — systems are being 'baselined' against irrelevant criteria while actual cloud-specific attack surfaces (IMDSv1 misconfiguration, overprivileged IAM roles, open security groups) go unaddressed. A baseline must be maintained and updated as the environment evolves, incorporating current threats and technology
Baselines should be recreated every week to stay current
The auditor should accept the baseline as-is since the company completed the migration
scenarios.exp: Baseline currency: a baseline that doesn't match the current environment gives false assurance — systems pass the 'baseline check' on irrelevant criteria while real vulnerabilities go unmeasured. Baselines must evolve with the environment, be reviewed after major changes (cloud migration, new technology), and incorporate current framework guidance (CIS Benchmarks release new versions as threats evolve).

scenarios.q: A security team uses SCAP content to automatically assess workstation compliance against CIS Benchmark Level 2 for Windows 10. The scan reports 91% compliance across 500 workstations. The team marks this as 'acceptable' and moves on. A security architect reviews and disagrees. Why?
opts: 91% compliance is excellent — the architect is being too strict

91% means 9% of controls fail across 500 workstations — that could be 45 workstations each missing a single control, or 5 workstations each missing 9 controls. The team needs to understand WHICH controls are failing and on WHICH systems. A single critical control failure (e.g., BitLocker disabled on a laptop) on 5 systems is more serious than 45 minor UI setting deviations. Aggregate compliance percentages hide individual risk — the team must review the specific failures
The SCAP tool is not approved for this use
CIS Benchmark Level 2 is too strict for workstations
scenarios.exp: Compliance percentage analysis: aggregate numbers mask individual risk. The right question is: what specifically is failing? A 9% non-compliance rate on trivial UI settings is different from 9% non-compliance on encryption or patching controls. Security teams must triage by control criticality, not just percentage. A single critical control failure on 5 systems may be more urgent than 100 minor configuration deviations.

cram.def: Change default credentials, disable unused ports, use SSH not Telnet, update firmware.
Minimal OS install, disable unnecessary services, patch promptly, host-based firewall.
Air gap where possible, patch carefully (availability priority), segment from corporate network.
MDM enrollment, container separation of corporate/personal data, remote wipe capability.
Network segmentation is most important — isolate in separate VLAN.

matching.prompt: Hardening first step — network devices
Encrypted admin for network devices
Air gap or strict segmentation for

questions.q: When hardening a new network switch, the FIRST action should be:
opts: Enable all available features
Configure VLANs
Change all default credentials
Update the switch firmware
questions.exp: Default credentials are publicly known — changing them immediately is the critical first hardening step.

questions.q: ICS/SCADA hardening differs from standard IT hardening because:
opts: ICS systems use stronger encryption
Availability is the top priority — patches and downtime must be planned carefully to avoid disrupting critical processes
ICS systems never need patching
ICS systems cannot be networked
questions.exp: In ICS/SCADA, disruption of physical processes is unacceptable — security changes must be planned around operational windows.

questions.q: Which hardening technique is most important for IoT devices with limited security features?
opts: Installing endpoint protection software
Network segmentation — isolating IoT devices in a separate VLAN away from critical systems
Full disk encryption
Deploying a host-based firewall
questions.exp: IoT devices often cannot be hardened directly — isolating them in a separate network segment limits the damage if they're compromised.

scenarios.q: A healthcare organization connects medical imaging devices (MRI, CT scanners) to the hospital network. These devices run Windows XP Embedded with no available patches, cannot have antivirus installed, and must be available 24/7. A security engineer proposes a compensating control strategy. Which combination BEST reduces risk without disrupting clinical operations?
opts: Disconnect all imaging devices from the network permanently
Install antivirus on the Windows XP systems using a third-party solution

Network segmentation placing imaging devices on an isolated VLAN with strict firewall rules permitting only the specific ports required for imaging workflows (DICOM protocol to the PACS server), combined with enhanced monitoring of all traffic to/from the imaging VLAN for anomalies
Upgrade all devices to Windows 10 before reconnecting to the network
scenarios.exp: IoT/medical device hardening via compensating controls: devices that cannot be patched or have AV installed must be protected at the network level. VLAN isolation limits what the device can reach if compromised; strict firewall rules limit inbound attack surface; DICOM-specific rules ensure only legitimate imaging traffic is permitted. Monitoring detects anomalies. This is the standard approach for unhardenable embedded systems.

scenarios.q: A company deploys a fleet of Android tablets for retail employees to process customer transactions. The tablets are enrolled in MDM, which enforces screen lock, encryption, and prevents sideloading. An employee reports their tablet was left unlocked on a counter and a customer may have accessed it. The security team reviews MDM logs. What hardening control would have automatically protected the tablet after the employee walked away?
opts: Remote wipe — the device should have been wiped immediately

Screen auto-lock with a short timeout (e.g., 30 seconds of inactivity) enforced by MDM policy — an unlocked tablet left unattended automatically locks itself, requiring re-authentication before the next transaction. This is a fundamental mobile device hardening control that doesn't rely on employee behavior
Full disk encryption — encrypted tablets cannot be accessed by customers
A more complex PIN — the customer couldn't have guessed a complex PIN
scenarios.exp: Auto-lock timeout: the simplest and most effective control for unattended device scenarios. MDM-enforced auto-lock after 30 seconds means even if an employee forgets to lock manually, the device locks itself. No amount of encryption or complex PINs helps if the device is left unlocked — the auto-lock is the last line of defense for physical access scenarios.

scenarios.q: A penetration tester is assessing a company's network device security. They use the default credentials 'admin/admin' to log into a core switch, then 'cisco/cisco' to access a router, and find a firewall still accessible on its management interface from the internet using the vendor default username and password. The company's IT team says these are 'internal devices' and assumed they were secure. What does this assessment demonstrate?
opts: The penetration tester used advanced techniques — these attacks cannot be prevented

Default credential attacks are the most trivially executed class of attack — they require no technical skill, only the ability to find vendor default credentials in publicly available documentation. 'Internal' doesn't mean 'secure from an attacker who has compromised any internal system.' Every network device must have its default credentials changed before production deployment, and management interfaces must not be accessible from untrusted network segments
The company should have used a VPN to protect management interfaces
Default credentials are acceptable for internal devices not facing the internet
scenarios.exp: Default credentials are trivially exploitable: vendor defaults are published online. Any attacker with internal network access (via phishing, physical access, or supply chain) immediately tries defaults. 'Internal only' is not a security boundary — it's a network constraint that can be crossed. Changing defaults is step zero of device hardening, non-negotiable for every device regardless of network position.

cram.def: Physical survey to plan AP placement, measure signal strength, identify interference.
Company owns device but allows personal use. Better control than BYOD.
Employee selects from company-approved device list. Company manages selected device.
Restricts device functionality based on geographic location.
Manages individual applications rather than the whole device — more granular than MDM.

matching.prompt: Physical survey planning AP placement
Company owns device, allows personal use
Employee chooses from approved device list
Restricts device based on geographic location

questions.q: A wireless site survey is performed to:
opts: Test network speed only
Plan optimal access point placement and measure signal coverage before and after deployment
Scan for rogue access points
Test wireless encryption strength
questions.exp: Site surveys map signal strength, identify interference, and optimize AP placement for coverage, performance, and security.

questions.q: COPE (Corporate-Owned Personally Enabled) differs from BYOD in that:
opts: COPE allows any personal device
The company owns the COPE device — giving greater control than BYOD while allowing limited personal use
COPE devices cannot have personal apps
COPE is more expensive than BYOD always
questions.exp: COPE: company buys and owns the device, allowing personal use. BYOD: employee's own device. COPE provides more corporate control.

questions.q: Geofencing is used in mobile device management to:
opts: Track device GPS for asset management only
Restrict device functionality or access based on geographic location — blocking corporate access outside defined areas
Improve wireless signal strength
Monitor battery usage
questions.exp: Geofencing can disable corporate apps or access when the device leaves defined geographic boundaries.

scenarios.q: A company's BYOD policy allows employees to use personal smartphones for work email and calendar access via a corporate MDM profile. An employee is dismissed for cause and their access is revoked. The IT team uses MDM to perform a 'selective wipe,' removing the corporate MDM profile and associated data. The employee calls to confirm their personal photos and apps are unaffected. What should the IT team confirm?
opts: All data has been wiped — selective wipe removes everything for security

The selective wipe removed only the corporate MDM container — corporate email, calendar, contacts, and corporate app data were removed, but personal photos, apps, and data outside the MDM profile are untouched. This is the designed purpose of MDM containerization for BYOD — corporate data can be removed without touching personal data
The employee must factory reset their device to verify
The MDM logs will show which files were deleted
scenarios.exp: MDM selective wipe for BYOD: containerization separates corporate and personal data. Selective wipe removes only the corporate container. This is the legal and practical compromise for BYOD — employees accept MDM to access corporate resources, but the organization commits to only touching corporate data. Full device wipe on personal devices raises legal issues; selective wipe on containerized data does not.

scenarios.q: A sales team uses COPE (Corporate-Owned Personally Enabled) smartphones. The company pays for the devices and plans, allows personal use, and manages them via MDM. A salesperson notices the MDM can track their GPS location continuously. They raise a privacy concern. The IT team explains this is necessary for asset tracking. What is the BEST policy response that balances security and employee privacy?
opts: Continuous GPS tracking is required and employees must accept it
Disable GPS tracking entirely — it creates too much legal risk

Configure MDM location tracking to be active only during business hours (or disable it entirely on COPE devices, relying on device remote wipe as the primary asset recovery tool), document the tracking policy clearly in the acceptable use agreement, and ensure employees acknowledge it at enrollment. For COPE devices used for personal activities outside work hours, continuous location tracking is disproportionate to the security benefit
Replace COPE with BYOD — BYOD gives employees more privacy
scenarios.exp: COPE privacy policy: employees bring personal lives to corporate devices in COPE programs. Continuous GPS tracking of off-hours location is a significant privacy intrusion with limited security benefit (remote wipe achieves device control without tracking). The appropriate control: time-limited or opt-in tracking, transparent AUP acknowledgement, and proportionate controls (remote wipe > constant surveillance). This is increasingly a legal requirement in jurisdictions with strong employee privacy laws.

scenarios.q: During a wireless site survey of a new office space, the survey team discovers significant RF interference in the 2.4GHz band from neighboring tenant equipment, but clean spectrum in the 5GHz band. The team also finds three dead zones near the server room where the metal walls attenuate signal. What should the site survey report recommend?
opts: Deploy all APs on 2.4GHz since it has better range

Deploy APs on 5GHz to avoid the interference from neighboring tenants, add APs or adjust placement to eliminate dead zones near the server room, and document the physical layout and RF measurements for future reference. Consider band steering to direct capable devices to 5GHz while maintaining 2.4GHz for legacy devices that don't support 5GHz
Use fewer APs with higher transmit power to compensate for dead zones
Install a Faraday cage around the server room to eliminate the attenuation
scenarios.exp: Site survey findings drive design: the survey exists to reveal exactly these issues before deployment. 5GHz recommendation addresses the interference; additional AP placement addresses dead zones. Site surveys are not just signal strength maps — they inform AP count, placement, channel planning, and band selection decisions. Deploying without acting on survey findings defeats the purpose.

cram.def: Latest wireless security. SAE replaces PSK. Resistant to offline attacks. Perfect forward secrecy.
Pre-shared key (PSK). Single password for all users.
802.1X with RADIUS. Per-user authentication. Unique session keys per user.
WPA3 replacement for PSK. Resistant to offline dictionary attacks.
Requires certificates on both client and server. Strongest EAP method.
8-digit PIN can be brute-forced in ~11,000 attempts. Disable WPS entirely.

matching.prompt: WPA3 replacement for PSK
Per-user authentication using RADIUS
EAP type requiring certificates on both sides
Single PSK shared by all users

questions.q: WPA3-Personal improves on WPA2-Personal primarily because:
opts: It uses longer passwords
SAE replaces PSK — providing resistance to offline dictionary attacks and perfect forward secrecy
It uses AES-256 instead of AES-128
It requires certificates
questions.exp: WPA3's SAE protocol prevents offline cracking of captured handshakes and provides PFS.

questions.q: WPA2-Enterprise is more secure than WPA2-Personal because:
opts: It uses stronger AES encryption
Per-user authentication via 802.1X/RADIUS — each user has unique credentials and unique session keys
It is faster
It doesn't require any passwords
questions.exp: Enterprise: each user authenticates individually — no shared PSK. Unique session keys per user improve isolation.

questions.q: Disabling WPS on wireless access points mitigates:
opts: Evil twin attacks
Deauthentication attacks
Brute-force attacks against the WPS PIN (which has a fundamental design flaw allowing ~11,000 attempts)
Encryption downgrade attacks
questions.exp: WPS PIN can be brute-forced in ~11,000 attempts due to its design — disabling WPS entirely is the only mitigation.

scenarios.q: A company is migrating their wireless network from WPA2-Personal to WPA2-Enterprise. The project manager asks why the migration takes months rather than days, given it's 'just a different authentication method.' What does the migration actually require?
opts: WPA2-Enterprise requires faster access points that must be purchased

WPA2-Enterprise requires 802.1X infrastructure: a RADIUS server (or cloud RADIUS service), certificate infrastructure (for EAP-TLS) or LDAP/AD integration (for PEAP), client supplicant configuration on every device (typically via GPO for Windows, MDM for mobile), and user training. Each device must be configured to use the correct EAP method and trust the authentication server certificate. This is an identity infrastructure project, not just a wireless setting change
WPA2-Enterprise requires replacing all access points
The migration is simple — it just requires updating the SSID password
scenarios.exp: WPA2-Enterprise migration complexity: the 'just change authentication' framing understates the infrastructure required. RADIUS server deployment, PKI for certificates, supplicant configuration at scale across all devices (requiring GPO, MDM, or manual configuration), and potential user training are all real project components. Project managers need accurate scoping — underestimating this leads to incomplete deployments where some devices fall back to WPA2-Personal.

scenarios.q: A wireless network uses WPA2-Personal with a pre-shared key. An employee who is leaving the company is concerned about retaining access after departure. The IT team rotates the PSK. Three other employees immediately call the helpdesk because their devices stopped connecting. What architectural limitation does this reveal, and what is the long-term solution?
opts: The PSK was set incorrectly — check the configuration

WPA2-Personal has a fundamental architectural limitation: all users share the same PSK. Revoking one user's access requires changing the key for everyone, then reconfiguring all devices. The long-term solution is WPA2-Enterprise with 802.1X — each user authenticates with individual credentials. Revoking access means disabling that user's account in AD/RADIUS, which immediately prevents their wireless access without affecting any other user
WPA2-Personal should use a longer PSK to prevent this issue
The employee should be physically prevented from accessing the building instead
scenarios.exp: PSK rekeying impact is a WPA2-Personal design limitation: one key for everyone means any access change affects everyone. This is why Enterprise authentication exists — per-user credentials allow per-user revocation. The helpdesk calls after a PSK rotation are a real operational pain that organizations regularly experience, and it's a compelling business argument for migrating to 802.1X.

scenarios.q: A red team discovers that a company's wireless network uses EAP-PEAP for authentication. They set up a rogue access point broadcasting the same SSID without a server certificate validation requirement. A Windows laptop connects to the rogue AP and attempts PEAP authentication. The rogue AP captures the user's username and a MSCHAPV2 challenge-response. What attack is being conducted, and what configuration change would have prevented the credential capture?
opts: A WPS PIN attack — disable WPS on all access points

An evil twin PEAP credential capture: when clients don't validate the server certificate, they'll authenticate against any AP claiming the correct SSID. The MSCHAPV2 response can be cracked offline. Prevention: configure clients to validate the server certificate and reject authentication if the certificate is not from the trusted corporate CA — this is done via supplicant configuration (GPO on Windows) so clients refuse to authenticate to rogue APs presenting unexpected certificates
A deauthentication attack — enable management frame protection
An RFID cloning attack — use certificate-based authentication instead
scenarios.exp: PEAP without certificate validation: PEAP's server certificate is the client's way to verify it's connecting to the legitimate RADIUS server. Without validation, any rogue AP can intercept PEAP authentication. The fix is supplicant configuration — tell Windows/mobile devices which CA to trust for the RADIUS server certificate, and refuse connection if the certificate doesn't match. This is a deployment step many organizations skip, leaving them vulnerable to this exact attack.

cram.def: Verifying all user input before processing. Whitelist (accept known good) preferred over blacklist.
Analyzing source code without executing it. Finds vulnerabilities early in development.
Testing running application. Sends inputs, observes behavior. Finds runtime vulnerabilities.
Sending random/unexpected input to find crashes and vulnerabilities.
Digitally signing application code — verifies publisher identity and detects tampering.
Security integrated into every phase of software development.

matching.prompt: Verifying user input before processing
Testing running application with attack inputs
Analyzing source code without running it
Sending random inputs to find crashes
Digitally signing software to verify publisher

questions.q: Input validation is the PRIMARY defense against:
opts: DDoS attacks
Injection attacks (SQL injection, XSS, command injection) by ensuring user input cannot be interpreted as code
Brute force password attacks
Physical security threats
questions.exp: Validating input prevents malicious data from being processed as code — the root cause of most injection vulnerabilities.

questions.q: SAST differs from DAST in that:
opts: SAST is more expensive
SAST analyses source code without execution — finding vulnerabilities early in development; DAST tests the running application
SAST is always automated; DAST is always manual
DAST finds more vulnerabilities than SAST
questions.exp: SAST = source code analysis (no execution). DAST = running app testing (finds runtime issues SAST misses).

questions.q: Fuzzing involves:
opts: Manually reviewing code for vulnerabilities
Sending random, unexpected, or malformed inputs to an application to discover crashes and security vulnerabilities
Scanning for known vulnerability signatures
Testing network protocols
questions.exp: Fuzzing automates vulnerability discovery by bombarding the application with unexpected inputs — finding edge cases developers miss.

scenarios.q: A developer submits code to a repository. The CI/CD pipeline automatically runs SAST, dependency vulnerability scanning (SCA), and unit tests. The SAST tool flags a potential SQL injection in a new API endpoint. The pipeline fails and the code is blocked from merging. The developer argues the finding is a false positive. What is the correct process?
opts: Allow the code to merge since the developer says it's a false positive

The security team reviews the flagged code with the developer — if confirmed as a false positive, the specific finding is suppressed with documented justification; if confirmed as a real vulnerability, the developer fixes it before merging. The key principle is that security gates in CI/CD pipelines must not be bypassed based solely on developer assertion — all security findings require review and documented disposition before code can merge
Override the pipeline and merge immediately to avoid development delays
Remove SAST from the pipeline — false positives slow development
scenarios.exp: Security gate process: developer assertion of false positive is the starting point for investigation, not the end. SAST findings must be reviewed and formally dispositioned — confirmed real vulnerabilities are fixed; confirmed false positives are suppressed with documentation. Bypassing security gates on assertion alone defeats the purpose of automated security testing and creates liability if the vulnerability turns out to be real.

scenarios.q: A web application accepts file uploads. Users can upload profile pictures. The developer validates that uploaded files have image extensions (.jpg, .png, .gif) by checking the file extension in the filename. A penetration tester uploads a file named 'shell.php.jpg' — the server accepts it because the extension is .jpg, but the web server executes it as PHP when accessed. What input validation failure occurred, and what is the correct fix?
opts: The file extension check was performed correctly — the server has a misconfiguration

Extension-only validation is insufficient — attackers control the filename. The correct fix is multi-layered: (1) validate the MIME type by inspecting the file's magic bytes (the actual file header, not the extension), (2) strip the filename and rename uploaded files server-side to a generated safe name with the correct extension, (3) store uploaded files outside the web root or in a separate domain so they cannot be executed by the web server, (4) configure the web server to not execute scripts in the upload directory
Block the .php extension in the filename blacklist
Require users to prove they are uploading real images by previewing them
scenarios.exp: File upload validation depth: extension checking is easily bypassed. Actual content validation (magic bytes), server-side renaming (remove attacker control of filename), storage outside web root (prevent execution), and server configuration (no script execution in upload directories) are all required layers. A file named shell.php.jpg that gets stored as shell.php.jpg in the web root and has PHP execution enabled is a web shell — a complete application takeover.

scenarios.q: A security team is performing a code review and finds the following in a Python Flask application: 'query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + user_id' where user_id comes from a URL parameter. They also find the application displays full Python stack traces in the browser on errors, and the debug mode is set to True in production. Rank these three findings by severity.
opts: Stack traces are most severe, debug mode is medium, SQL concatenation is low

SQL injection via string concatenation is critical (allows database extraction or destruction); debug mode in production is high (enables the Werkzeug debugger which allows arbitrary code execution via the browser); verbose stack traces are medium (information disclosure revealing code structure, file paths, and library versions). Fix order: disable debug mode immediately (active RCE risk), fix SQL injection (use parameterized queries), then suppress error details in production responses
Debug mode is most severe since it enables the REPL
Stack traces are most severe since they reveal the entire codebase
scenarios.exp: Severity ranking for web app findings: debug mode with Werkzeug in production is a critical RCE risk — the interactive debugger allows arbitrary Python execution from the browser. SQL injection is critical but requires the specific vulnerable endpoint to be reached. Stack traces are information disclosure (serious but not directly exploitable). The exam expects students to reason about relative severity and impact, not just identify vulnerability classes.

cram.def: Repository of all IT asset information and relationships. Foundation for change and incident management.
Securely removing data from media before disposal or reuse. Methods: overwriting, degaussing, destruction.
Using magnetic field to erase magnetic media (HDD, tape). NOT effective on SSDs.
Shredding, pulverizing, incineration. Most thorough. Required for SSDs.
Document proving media was securely destroyed — required for compliance.
End of Life / End of Support Life — vendor stops providing security updates.

matching.prompt: Repository of all IT assets and their relationships
Magnetic field erasing magnetic media
Most thorough sanitization — guaranteed unrecoverable
Required for SSD sanitization
Vendor stops providing security patches

questions.q: Why is degaussing NOT effective for sanitizing SSDs?
opts: Degaussing is too slow for SSDs
SSDs use flash memory, not magnetic storage — a magnetic field has no effect on flash memory cells
Degaussing is too expensive for SSDs
SSDs are always encrypted, making degaussing unnecessary
questions.exp: Degaussing works on magnetic media. SSDs use NAND flash — degaussing has zero effect. Physical destruction is needed.

questions.q: Which media sanitization method provides the STRONGEST assurance of data unrecoverability?
opts: Overwriting with zeros
Degaussing
Physical destruction (shredding/incineration)
Formatting
questions.exp: Physical destruction guarantees media cannot be read — used for high-sensitivity data.

questions.q: An organization is disposing of old laptops with confidential data. The MOST appropriate action is:
opts: Simply deleting files and donating the laptops
Formatting the drives and reselling them
Securely wiping or physically destroying storage media and obtaining a certificate of destruction
Storing the laptops in a locked room indefinitely
questions.exp: Secure disposal requires verifiable sanitization and documentation (certificate of destruction) for compliance.

scenarios.q: An IT team discovers a server running critical financial reporting software that is not in the CMDB, has not received patches in 18 months, is not covered by any backup policy, and is only known to one employee who set it up before leaving the company. The server is running Windows Server 2008 R2 which reached end of life in January 2020. What risk does this represent, and what immediate actions are required?
opts: Low risk — the server has been running fine for 18 months without incident

This is a ghost/rogue asset representing multiple critical risks: (1) EOL OS with no security patches for years — known exploitable vulnerabilities will never be patched; (2) not in asset management so it receives no patching, monitoring, or backup coverage; (3) single point of knowledge creates operational risk. Immediate actions: add to CMDB, assess what data it holds and its network exposure, implement emergency compensating controls (network isolation, enhanced monitoring), engage the business to plan migration to a supported platform
The server should be immediately powered off
Remove the server from the network and ignore it since it's not officially managed
scenarios.exp: Ghost asset risk: undiscovered assets in production are blind spots — they receive no patching, monitoring, or backup coverage. An EOL OS compounds this with permanent unpatched vulnerabilities. The single-employee knowledge dependency creates an operational continuity risk independent of security. Asset discovery scanning should have found this — the fact that it didn't indicates gaps in asset management processes.

scenarios.q: A company is disposing of 200 decommissioned laptops. Some were used by executives and contain sensitive financial data. The IT team proposes reformatting the hard drives (quick format) before donating the laptops to a local school. A security officer objects. Why, and what is the correct disposal procedure?
opts: Quick format is sufficient — it deletes all user data

Quick format only removes file system references — the actual data remains on the drive and is recoverable with freely available tools. For laptops that contained sensitive financial data, the correct procedure is: (1) secure overwrite (DoD 5220.22-M or equivalent multi-pass overwrite) for functional drives, or (2) physical destruction (shredding) for maximum assurance, or (3) cryptographic erasure if the drives are encrypted. A certificate of destruction should be obtained for audit purposes
Donating laptops with corporate data is always illegal
The laptops should be physically destroyed regardless of data sensitivity
scenarios.exp: Data remanence after quick format: quick format (and even full format) leaves data recoverable. The FAT/NTFS structures are reset but sectors are not overwritten. For drives containing sensitive data, overwriting or destruction is required. The appropriate method depends on data sensitivity — executives' financial data warrants at minimum secure overwrite and potentially physical destruction. Donating un-sanitized drives is a data breach.

scenarios.q: During an audit, an assessor finds that the organization has 847 active software licenses tracked in the CMDB, but vulnerability scans identify 1,243 unique software installations across the network. The delta of 396 unlicensed installations includes several applications with known critical vulnerabilities. What does this demonstrate, and what control addresses it?
opts: The vulnerability scanner is producing false positives

Shadow IT and software asset management gaps: 396 applications are installed but not tracked in the CMDB — meaning they receive no patch management, no license compliance management, and no vulnerability tracking. The critical vulnerabilities in untracked software may never be remediated. Application allow-listing (only approved software can run) combined with comprehensive software asset management closes this gap by preventing installation of unapproved software
Unlicensed software is a legal concern only, not a security concern
The CMDB needs to be updated to add the 396 applications as approved
scenarios.exp: Software asset management security impact: untracked software isn't just a license compliance issue — it's a security gap. Untracked applications aren't included in patch management cycles, so vulnerabilities go undetected and unpatched indefinitely. Allow-listing prevents installation of unapproved software; CMDB tracking ensures approved software is covered by patch management. The 396 gap represents 396 potential attack vectors with no remediation path.

cram.def: Automated tool identifying known vulnerabilities. Examples: Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS.
Scanner logs into systems — deeper, more accurate results. Finds local vulnerabilities.
External perspective — what an unauthenticated attacker sees. Finds exposed services.
Reported vulnerability that doesn't actually exist. Wastes remediation effort.
Real vulnerability not detected. Dangerous — gives false confidence.

matching.prompt: Scanner authenticates to systems for deeper results
External unauthenticated perspective
Real vulnerability not detected by scanner
Reported vulnerability that doesn't exist

questions.q: A credentialed vulnerability scan provides more accurate results because:
opts: It scans more IP addresses
The scanner authenticates to systems — finding local vulnerabilities, missing patches, and misconfigurations invisible externally
It uses more attack signatures
It runs faster
questions.exp: Credentialed scans see what's inside the system — local patch status, user rights, registry settings — not just externally exposed services.

questions.q: False negatives in vulnerability scanning are MORE dangerous than false positives because:
opts: False negatives are easier to fix
A false negative means a real vulnerability was missed — giving false confidence while the vulnerability remains exploitable
False positives waste less time
They both have equal impact on security
questions.exp: False negatives are the unseen danger — you think you're safe but the vulnerability is real and exploitable.

scenarios.q: A security team runs a credentialed vulnerability scan of their web servers and gets 847 findings. The same servers scanned without credentials produce 23 findings. The team has been reporting the non-credentialed scan results to management as their vulnerability posture. What is wrong with this approach, and why does the gap between 23 and 847 findings exist?
opts: The credentialed scan is producing false positives — use the non-credentialed results

The non-credentialed scan only shows externally visible vulnerabilities (open ports, exposed services, banner information) — what an unauthenticated attacker from outside sees. The credentialed scan authenticates to the server and sees everything an insider or attacker with credentials would see: unpatched OS components, vulnerable locally-installed software, misconfigured services, weak file permissions. Reporting only non-credentialed results drastically underrepresents actual risk — most vulnerabilities are internal and invisible to unauthenticated scans
The credentialed scan has a bug — contact the vendor
Both scan types are unnecessary — use a WAF instead
scenarios.exp: Credentialed vs non-credentialed scope: the 824-finding gap represents real vulnerabilities that exist but are invisible to an external scanner. Most software vulnerabilities (unpatched OS components, third-party apps) are not exposed externally — they require authenticated access to discover. Organizations that report only non-credentialed scan results give management a false picture of security posture.

scenarios.q: A vulnerability scanner reports a critical finding on a server: 'OpenSSL 1.0.1e — Remote Code Execution (CVE-XXXX-XXXX).' The system administrator says the finding is a false positive because they manually upgraded OpenSSL three weeks ago. The scanner is still reporting the old version. What is most likely happening, and what should be verified?
opts: The scanner is correct — the administrator is wrong

The scanner may be reading the OpenSSL version from a service banner or registry entry that wasn't updated after the manual upgrade, or there may be multiple versions of OpenSSL installed and the scanner found the old one still present. Verification: check the actual installed OpenSSL binary version ('openssl version'), check for multiple OpenSSL library versions on the filesystem, verify the service using OpenSSL is actually linked against the new version, and confirm the old binary/library files were removed during the upgrade
The scanner should be reconfigured to trust administrator assertions
Mark all findings on this server as false positives
scenarios.exp: False positive investigation process: scanner findings on patched vulnerabilities require verification, not automatic dismissal. Manual upgrades can leave old binaries in non-standard locations; scanners may read version info from sources that weren't updated. Verification must be done against the actual running binary and the actual library the service loads — not just what was installed. This is a common real-world scenario the exam tests through vulnerability management scenarios.

cram.def: Evidence-based knowledge about threats — TTPs, IoCs, threat actors.
Open Source Intelligence — publicly available information.
Information Sharing and Analysis Center — sector-specific threat sharing organizations.
Standardized formats for sharing threat intelligence between organizations.
Proactive searching for threats — assumes breach, looks for hidden adversary activity.

matching.prompt: Publicly available threat information
Sector-specific threat sharing organization
Standardized threat intel sharing format
Proactive searching assuming compromise

questions.q: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) uses:
opts: Classified government information
Publicly available sources including news sites, social media, vulnerability databases, and public forums
Only commercial threat feeds
Only dark web sources
questions.exp: OSINT leverages openly available information — no special access required.

questions.q: Threat hunting differs from reactive security monitoring in that:
opts: Threat hunting uses automated tools; monitoring uses humans
Threat hunting assumes the organization is already compromised and proactively searches for hidden adversary activity
Threat hunting only looks at network traffic
Monitoring is more effective than threat hunting
questions.exp: Threat hunting is proactive — hunters look for stealthy attackers who have evaded automated detection.

scenarios.q: A financial institution's threat intelligence team receives a STIX feed containing indicators of compromise (IoCs) from a sector ISAC: 47 malicious IP addresses, 12 domain names, and 8 file hashes associated with a banking trojan actively targeting financial institutions. What is the MOST operationally valuable immediate action with these IoCs?
opts: Store them in a threat intelligence database for future reference

Ingest the IoCs into operational security controls immediately: add the 47 IP addresses to firewall blocklists and SIEM correlation rules, add the 12 domains to DNS filtering and proxy blocklists, distribute the 8 file hashes to EDR platforms for detection. Simultaneously check historical logs for any existing matches (past network connections to the IPs/domains, file hash matches in EDR telemetry) — a historical hit may indicate an existing compromise. Operational ingestion within hours of receiving threat intel is the goal
Publish the IoCs on the company website to warn other organizations
File a report with law enforcement about the threat
scenarios.exp: Threat intel operationalization: IoCs have a short shelf life — malicious infrastructure changes frequently. The value is in immediate ingestion into controls (firewall, DNS, EDR) and retrospective hunt (did we already see this?). Threat intel that sits in a database without being operationalized provides no protection. The ISAC sharing model exists specifically to enable rapid defensive action across the sector.

scenarios.q: A threat hunter reviews network logs and notices an employee's workstation has been connecting to a domain registered 4 days ago at regular 5-minute intervals, even outside business hours. The connections return small amounts of data. The employee reports no unusual activity. Threat intelligence feeds show no information about the domain. What threat hunting hypothesis should guide the investigation?
opts: The domain is likely a legitimate cloud service — no action needed

The pattern is consistent with malware beaconing: regular timed intervals (5-minute timer), newly registered domain (a common evasion technique since threat feeds haven't flagged it yet), activity outside business hours (when automated processes run unattended), small data returns (command polling rather than bulk exfiltration). Hunt: examine the process making the connection (EDR process tree), memory forensics of that process, DNS query history, and what was installed or executed around the time the beaconing started
The employee is violating acceptable use policy by accessing personal websites
The 5-minute interval indicates a legitimate software update check
scenarios.exp: Threat hunting methodology: the hypothesis drives the investigation. Beaconing behavior (regular intervals + new domain + out-of-hours + small data) is a well-documented C2 pattern. The absence of threat intel hits for a newly registered domain is itself suspicious — attackers register new domains specifically to avoid existing blocklists. EDR process investigation will identify what's making the connection; memory forensics may find the injected code.

scenarios.q: A company receives a threat intelligence report that a nation-state actor is targeting organizations in their industry using spear phishing emails with malicious Word documents. The TTPs include: initial access via phishing, living-off-the-land techniques (PowerShell, WMI), lateral movement using pass-the-hash, and exfiltration via DNS tunneling. The CISO asks which controls to prioritize. What is the correct prioritization?
opts: Block all PowerShell execution — this stops the attack chain entirely

Layered controls mapped to the attack chain: (1) anti-phishing (email gateway, DMARC, user training) to reduce initial access success rate; (2) PowerShell logging and constrained language mode to detect/limit LOLBin abuse; (3) network segmentation and credential hygiene to limit pass-the-hash lateral movement reach; (4) DNS monitoring for tunneling patterns and restricting direct internet DNS to the internal resolver. No single control stops the entire chain — the goal is to fail the attacker at multiple stages
Purchase cyber insurance — nation-state attacks cannot be defended against
Block all outbound traffic — this prevents exfiltration
scenarios.exp: TTP-mapped defensive prioritization: intelligence about adversary TTPs allows defenders to map controls to the kill chain. Each phase of the attack chain has corresponding defensive measures. The goal isn't to find one magic control — it's to make each stage harder, forcing the attacker to work harder and creating more detection opportunities. This is how threat intelligence drives strategic security decisions.

cram.def: Authorized simulated attack to identify and demonstrate exploitable vulnerabilities.
No prior knowledge of target. Simulates external attacker. Most realistic.
Full knowledge of target. Most thorough coverage.
Partial knowledge — simulates insider or attacker with some reconnaissance.
Document defining scope, timing, allowed techniques, and off-limits systems.
Offensive team simulating real attackers.
Red and blue teams working together — sharing findings in real-time.

matching.prompt: No prior knowledge — simulates external attacker
Full knowledge — most thorough coverage
Defines scope, timing, and rules before testing
Red and blue working together in real-time

questions.q: Penetration testing differs from vulnerability scanning in that:
opts: Pen testing uses automated tools; scanning uses humans
Pen testing actively exploits vulnerabilities to demonstrate real impact; scanning only identifies potential vulnerabilities
Scanning is more comprehensive than pen testing
Pen testing is always automated
questions.exp: Vulnerability scanning identifies potential weaknesses. Pen testing actually exploits them — proving they're real and demonstrating business impact.

questions.q: Rules of Engagement (RoE) define:
opts: The payment terms for the test
The scope, timing, permitted techniques, and off-limits systems — legally protecting both tester and organization
The reporting format
The vulnerabilities to be found
questions.exp: RoE establishes the legal framework — preventing the tester from being prosecuted and the organization from unexpected disruption.

questions.q: A purple team exercise is most valuable for:
opts: Finding vulnerabilities the red team missed
Having red and blue teams collaborate in real-time — sharing findings immediately to improve detection capabilities
Reducing the cost of security testing
Simulating nation-state attacks
questions.exp: Purple teaming: red shares attack techniques, blue improves detection. Both teams learn and defenses improve immediately.

scenarios.q: A penetration tester has been engaged for a black box test. They conduct passive reconnaissance for two weeks, discovering the company's IP ranges, employee names and titles from LinkedIn, email format from a data breach database, and a forgotten subdomain (dev.company.com) hosting a staging version of the application. They haven't touched any of the company's systems yet. What phase are they in, and why is this phase valuable even before any active testing?
opts: Active reconnaissance — touching systems has already begun

Passive reconnaissance: all information was gathered from public sources without touching the target's systems. This phase is valuable because it replicates what real attackers do before launching attacks — attackers don't immediately start scanning, they first understand the target. The forgotten dev subdomain is a significant finding (staging environments often have weaker controls and real production data). This intelligence shapes the entire active test — the tester now knows where to focus, what credentials to try, and which systems may be more vulnerable
Exploitation — the subdomain is being attacked
Social engineering — using LinkedIn data directly constitutes an attack
scenarios.exp: Passive recon phase value: attackers invest significant time in passive recon before touching a target. The dev subdomain discovery through DNS enumeration and Google dorking is a zero-touch finding that real attackers regularly exploit. Pen testers who skip passive recon miss findings that may be the most critical — forgotten systems with no monitoring, exposed development environments, and intelligence that dramatically improves active phase effectiveness.

scenarios.q: A penetration test engagement has Rules of Engagement specifying: scope includes the company's production web application (192.168.1.0/24), testing window is weekdays 9am-5pm, destructive testing is prohibited, and social engineering requires written CISO approval for each campaign. During testing, the tester discovers a server at 192.168.2.50 that appears to be a domain controller — outside the defined scope but potentially accessible. What should the tester do?
opts: Test the domain controller immediately — it's too valuable a finding to ignore

Stop testing the out-of-scope system and immediately notify the client in writing that a potentially significant system was discovered adjacent to the scope boundary. The client decides whether to expand scope through a written amendment to the Rules of Engagement. Testing out-of-scope systems without explicit authorization — even in a penetration test — is unauthorized computer access. The RoE is the legal document protecting both parties
Note the finding and include it in the final report without testing it
Test the domain controller after hours to avoid the testing window restriction
scenarios.exp: RoE as legal boundary: the RoE defines what is authorized. Testing out-of-scope systems — even during an authorized penetration test — is not covered by the engagement authorization and could constitute unauthorized access. The tester's professional and legal obligation is to notify the client and await explicit written authorization to expand scope. This is a real ethical and legal distinction the exam tests.

scenarios.q: A company conducts an annual penetration test that finds the same 12 critical vulnerabilities year after year. The CISO argues the penetration test program is broken. The security team argues they've been patching but the vulnerabilities reappear. What does this pattern indicate, and what root cause should be investigated?
opts: The penetration testing firm is incompetent — hire a new firm

The recurring findings indicate a systemic remediation failure: either (1) patching is applied to discovered instances but not to the root cause (e.g., the vulnerable software version is in a base image and every new deployment creates new instances), (2) the change management process isn't capturing all systems in scope for patching, or (3) the vulnerability is being marked as remediated without verification. Root cause analysis should examine: why are these vulnerabilities recurring — is it a build/deployment process issue, a patch management gap, or a false closure process?
The vulnerabilities are unfixable — accept them as residual risk
Switch from annual pen testing to quarterly scanning only
scenarios.exp: Recurring pen test findings as process indicators: the same vulnerabilities year after year indicate a systemic issue, not just a patching backlog. Common causes: vulnerable base images (new deployments start vulnerable), patch management not covering all systems, or inadequate remediation validation. Root cause analysis of recurring findings is more valuable than the findings themselves — fix the process, not just the instances.

cram.def: Common Vulnerability Scoring System. 0-10 severity score. 9.0-10 = Critical.
Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures. Public database with CVE-YYYY-XXXXX identifiers.
National Vulnerability Database. NIST augments CVE with CVSS scores and patch info.
Exploit Prediction Scoring System. Probability a vulnerability will be exploited in the wild.

matching.prompt: 0-10 vulnerability severity score
Public database with CVE-YYYY-XXXXX identifiers
Probability vulnerability will be exploited
CVSS score for Critical severity

questions.q: CVSS is used to:
opts: Identify specific vulnerabilities
Provide a standardized 0-10 severity score to prioritize vulnerability remediation
Patch vulnerabilities automatically
Monitor network traffic for exploits
questions.exp: CVSS gives each vulnerability a standardized score (0-10) — organizations use scores to prioritize which vulnerabilities to fix first.

questions.q: A CVSS score of 9.5 indicates:
opts: Low severity
Medium severity
High severity
Critical severity — patch immediately
questions.exp: CVSS 9.0-10.0 = Critical. These require immediate remediation.

questions.q: EPSS improves vulnerability prioritization by:
opts: Replacing CVSS
Adding the probability that a vulnerability will be actively exploited in the wild — helping focus on likely real-world threats
